title: runtime: export scheduler lag + queue depth metrics for overload protection
preview:
Context: Hot-route capacity protection needs a small set of runtime-originated pressure signals that are (a) cheap to compute in the data plane, (b) stable under high load, and (c) low-cardinality enough to be safely shipped at high frequency. This PR adds two primary signals to the runtime metrics pipeline: scheduler lag (time between eligible-to-run and actually scheduled) and queue depth (pending work items) with limited dimensionality.

What’s in this PR: 1) Scheduler lag histogram (ms) emitted at the scheduling boundary per engine instance. Lag is computed as (now - enqueue_ts) at the moment the request enters a runnable slot. 2) Queue depth gauge emitted from the admission queue, representing pending requests waiting for runtime scheduling. 3) Optional route_group attribution: runtime does NOT attach raw route/path; it accepts a pre-aggregated route_group identifier from the router (small fixed set) and will emit per-route_group metrics only when explicitly enabled for protected routes. 4) Sampling + emission controls to keep overhead bounded: fixed-rate emission for gauges (default 1s), capped label sets, and a safety circuit that disables route_group attribution if distinct values exceed a threshold.

Why: These metrics are used by the routing/admission control layer (ENG-4924) to gate best-effort traffic earlier, and by SRE dashboards/alerts (ENG-4926) to understand when load shedding / admission control is expected vs when tail latency is caused by other runtime regressions.

Key design points: - Low overhead: all hot-path math is integer/monotonic clock only; no string formatting in the scheduling loop. - Low cardinality: label set is strictly bounded (region, pool, model_family, route_group when enabled). No tenant/customer labels. - Deterministic meaning: scheduler_lag_ms measures time waiting specifically for runtime scheduling capacity, not network queueing or router queueing.

Metrics (names + labels): - redwood.runtime.scheduler_lag_ms (histogram) labels: region, gpu_pool, model_family, route_group(optional) - redwood.runtime.queue_depth (gauge) labels: region, gpu_pool, model_family, route_group(optional) - redwood.runtime.overload_signal_enabled (gauge) labels: region, gpu_pool (1/0)

Operational notes: - Default: route_group attribution disabled. Router team will enable only for the protected route groups via config in a follow-up PR, after validating cardinality in staging. - If route_group attribution is enabled and the runtime observes >32 distinct route_group values in a 5m window, it will automatically drop route_group labeling and emit a structured warning log once per window.

Testing: - Unit tests validate lag calculation and monotonic clock usage. - A micro-benchmark validates <0.3% CPU overhead at 10k req/s synthetic scheduler throughput with gauges at 1s and histogram sampling at 1/10. - Integration smoke in staging: dashboards show stable cardinality and no increased scrape payloads.

Rollout: - Behind flag RUNTIME_OVERLOAD_SIGNALS. - Staging enabled by default for 48h; prod canary in us-east on one pool after SRE sign-off.

Follow-ups: - Add burn-rate alert wiring in slo-toolkit once dashboards confirm stable behavior. - Align routing thresholds once we have a week of baseline distribution under normal traffic.

References: Linear ENG-4925, ENG-4924. Related: postmortem 2025-11-03 (fleetwide latency cascade).

title: Enable feature-flagged hot-route protection (us-east canary) + rollback guardrails
preview:
Context
This PR wires up progressive rollout controls for Hot-route capacity protection (tier-aware admission control / priority routing) and performs the first canary enablement in us-east.

We already have the core enforcement plumbing landing in:
- Gateway tier-aware rate limiting + 429 semantics: pr-27411 / pr-27441
- Policy engine priority classes + route budgets: pr-27418
- Routing admission control using capacity signals: pr-27427
- Runtime metrics export: pr-27435

What this PR adds is the rollout substrate: feature flags, allowlists, and a repeatable enable/disable workflow with guardrails.

Goals
- Default safe: all protection behavior OFF unless explicitly enabled for a region + account allowlist.
- Canary first: enable for a small set of enterprise accounts and the protected route group in us-east.
- Reversible: provide one-command rollback that disables protection at gateway + routing consistently.
- Auditable: log flag evaluations and canary activations (low-cardinality) for on-call.

Rollout model (high level)
- Flags are evaluated in control-plane config service and cached in data plane.
- Two gates must pass before protection activates:
  1) region gate: hot_route_protection.enabled_regions contains current region (e.g., us-east)
  2) account gate: request tenant_id is in hot_route_protection.allowlisted_tenants (or allowlist group)
- Optional third gate for route group: hot_route_protection.allowlisted_route_groups (default: protected_enterprise_hot_routes)

Flags introduced
- hot_route_protection.enabled (global master kill switch; default false)
- hot_route_protection.enabled_regions (default empty)
- hot_route_protection.allowlisted_tenants (default empty)
- hot_route_protection.allowlisted_route_groups (default empty)
- hot_route_protection.debug_headers (default false; when true, adds X-Redwood-Admission-Debug: <reason> for allowlisted internal tenants only)

Canary enablement in us-east
- Config seeded with:
  - enabled=false globally
  - enabled_regions=["us-east"]
  - allowlisted_route_groups=["protected_enterprise_hot_routes"]
  - allowlisted_tenants=[REDACTED: 6 enterprise tenant IDs tracked in ENG-4928]
- Note: we intentionally do NOT flip hot_route_protection.enabled in this PR. The enable step is in the release runbook window (see rollout instructions below). This PR only ensures the us-east canary config is present and validated.

Rollback updates
- Updates scripts/ops/hot_route_protection_{enable,disable}.sh to:
  - atomically set hot_route_protection.enabled=false
  - clear enabled_regions and allowlists (belt + suspenders)
  - verify propagation by polling config version + checking sample gateway/routing flag eval logs
- Adds scripts/ops/hot_route_protection_status.sh to print current config and last-applied change (from audit log)

Observability
- Adds two low-cardinality metrics:
  - controlplane.feature_flag_eval_total{flag_name, result}
  - dataplane.hot_route_protection_active_total{region, route_group, tier}
- Adds structured log line when protection becomes active per process: "hot_route_protection_active": true/false with {region, route_group_count}

Testing
- Unit tests for flag evaluation gates (region/account/route-group)
- Integration test (mocked config service) to ensure default-off behavior and correct activation for allowlisted tenant

Rollout instructions (for release owner)
1) Merge this PR
2) During scheduled window, run scripts/ops/hot_route_protection_enable.sh --region us-east --tenants <allowlist_file>
3) Monitor dashboards (ENG-4926) and incident-bot notifications (ENG-4927)
4) If any unexpected behavior, run scripts/ops/hot_route_protection_disable.sh immediately (target <2 min)

Notes / follow-ups
- INT-3302: Access provisioning for the (future) admission decision debug endpoint remains separate; this PR only adds internal-only debug header toggle.
- We should keep allowlisted_tenants list in config, not in code, to avoid redeploys for canary adjustments.

Refs
- Linear: ENG-4928, ENG-4926, ENG-4927
- Postmortem driver: 2025-11-03 Sev1 hot route cascade

title: Add perf-canary alert handler (Slack threads, dedupe, runbook links)
preview:
Summary: Adds a first-class handler in incident-bot for perf-canary regression alerts. The handler formats alerts into a consistent Slack thread, de-dupes repeated firings, and attaches deep links (Grafana dashboard + Confluence runbook + relevant traces) to speed triage.

Context: The perf-canary service will emit alerts as a webhook payload (model + region + tier + metric + observed vs baseline + confidence + baseline version/runtime tags). Before this PR, incident-bot treated canary payloads as generic webhooks which led to noisy, non-threaded posts and no reliable dedupe key, making it hard to triage during regressions and especially during baseline drift/threshold tuning.

What this PR does: 1) Adds a new webhook route + handler for source=perf-canary (Content-Type: application/json). 2) Builds a stable dedupe key and thread key so repeat evaluations (same model/metric/region/severity) update the same Slack thread instead of spamming the channel. 3) Produces a compact Slack message with: top-line regression summary; baseline vs observed; min-samples/confidence; tags (model_version/runtime_build/kernel_tag/hw_sku); and quick links. 4) Adds a lightweight state store interface for dedupe/threads (Redis-backed in prod; in-memory fake for tests). 5) Adds guardrails: drop oversized payload fields, strip unknown labels, and redact any prompt-like fields (defense-in-depth even though canary is synthetic).

Dedupe/threading behavior: - Dedupe key: perf_canary|<env>|<model_id>|<model_version>|<region>|<tier>|<metric>|<severity>|<baseline_id>|<runtime_build>|<hw_sku>. - Thread key: perf_canary|<env>|<model_id>|<region>|<tier>|<metric>|<severity>. - If a new alert matches an existing thread key within the configured TTL (default 6h), we post a reply in-thread and update the thread header (Slack message update) with the most recent observed values. - If severity escalates (warning -> page), we start/attach to the paging thread and include an explicit "Escalated" note with timestamps.

Links: - Runbook: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert (hardcoded base + slug; environment-aware). - Grafana: deep link to per-model dashboard with prefilled variables (model, region, tier, hw_sku) and time range anchored around first_seen. - Traces/logs: link templates for Tempo/Loki queries using request_route=perf-canary and model_id.

Testing: - Unit tests cover dedupe key generation, Slack block rendering, and TTL behavior. - Integration test covers "first alert creates thread" + "repeat alert updates thread" + "escalation".

Rollout: - Disabled by default behind flag incident_bot.perf_canary.enabled (default false). - Staging enabled for #eng-runtime-canary; prod enablement tracked in ENG-4878.

Checklist: - [x] Adds handler behind feature flag - [x] Slack formatting aligned with SRE paging hygiene - [x] Dedupe TTL configurable - [x] Runbook + dashboard links included - [x] Tests added - [x] Backward compatible with existing generic webhook handler

title: Add status summary metadata to incident events
preview:
Context
We’re implementing auto-drafted, publishable post-incident summaries (ENG-4823). The incident-bot can already export a timeline of events (see PR #19540), but the events don’t currently carry the structured metadata we need to generate consistent “Impact / Timeline / Mitigation” blocks without hand-parsing free-form notes.

This PR adds optional, structured “status summary” metadata to incident events emitted by the redwood control plane so incident-bot can draft post-incident summaries with fewer manual edits and lower risk of accidentally including sensitive detail.

What’s included
- Adds optional fields to incident event payloads:
  - impact_start (RFC3339 timestamp; when customer impact started, as determined by IC/SRE)
  - impact_end (RFC3339 timestamp; when customer impact ended / SLO recovered)
  - affected_routes (array of route identifiers, e.g. "chat.completions", "embeddings", "rerank"; intentionally coarse)
  - fallback_active (boolean; whether automatic fallback policy was active during the impact window)
- Extends the incident timeline emitter to attach these fields on relevant state transitions (Identified/Mitigating/Monitoring/Resolved) when present in the incident record.
- Adds validation/normalization:
  - impact_end must be >= impact_start
  - affected_routes is capped to a small allowlist and de-duplicated
  - timestamps are normalized to UTC
- Adds unit + golden tests for event serialization to keep incident-bot consumption stable.
- Adds a lightweight feature flag (incidents.emit_status_summary_metadata) default-off in production for staged rollout.

Non-goals
- No status page updates or incident-bot changes in this PR (those live in incident-bot / tooling PRs).
- No attempt to infer impact_start/end from metrics. These fields are explicitly set by IC/Comms Lead during incident handling.
- No inclusion of customer identifiers, tenant IDs, request IDs, or capacity details.

Implementation details
- Introduces StatusSummaryMetadata as a dedicated struct used by the incident domain model and event encoder.
- Affected routes are represented using our public API “capability” labels rather than internal service names to align with the status component taxonomy work.
- Backwards compatibility: existing consumers that ignore unknown JSON keys will continue to work.

Changed areas (high level)
- control-plane/incidents: incident model + validation
- control-plane/events: incident event encoder
- api/types: shared type definitions + docs
- tests: serialization + schema compatibility

Example event (illustrative)
{"type":"incident.state_changed","incident_id":"inc_01J...","state":"monitoring","status_summary":{"impact_start":"2025-11-24T18:12:00Z","impact_end":null,"affected_routes":["chat.completions","chat.stream"],"fallback_active":true}}

Rollout plan
1) Merge behind feature flag (this PR)
2) Enable in staging + dogfood incidents
3) Enable in prod for Sev0/Sev1 only (via config) once incident-bot parsing is deployed
4) Expand to all severities after 1–2 weeks of audits

Testing
- Unit tests for validation rules
- Golden tests for JSON payload stability
- Manual: toggled feature flag in dev environment and verified event payload contains status_summary and is omitted when disabled

Screenshots
N/A (backend-only)

Follow-ups
- incident-bot: consume these fields when drafting post-incident summary (linked to ENG-4823)
- docs: add a short internal note on how IC/Comms Lead should set impact_start/end (separate Confluence update)

Review conversation (highlights)
- Rafael Mendes: "Can we ensure affected_routes doesn’t leak internal endpoint names?" 
  - Logan Wright: "Yes — using public capability labels only; added allowlist + normalization and updated tests."
- Aisha Bello: "impact_end should be nullable until Resolved; do we want Monitoring to allow null?" 
  - Logan Wright: "Yes; Monitoring can still have null impact_end (we sometimes monitor after SLO recovers). Added explicit behavior + tests."
- Miles Thornton: "Feature flag default-off is good. Please also cap affected_routes length to avoid huge payloads." 
  - Logan Wright: "Done (max 10, de-dupe + stable ordering)."
- Christine Wong: "Can we keep fallback_active optional? Some incidents won’t have routing fallback." 
  - Logan Wright: "Kept optional; boolean only included when known, otherwise omitted."

CI
- build: pass
- unit tests: pass
- lint: pass

Outcome
Merged (squash) to main; feature flag remains off by default pending incident-bot deployment and SRE sign-off.

title: Anti-flap logic (windows + hysteresis) for status-component health signals
preview: Motivation: Our current component health rollups (used to recommend public status changes and to populate status-page dashboards) are too twitchy. Short-lived spikes (e.g., 2–3 minutes of elevated 5xx or a brief p95 regression) can flip a component between Operational ↔ Degraded multiple times, which creates noisy on-call workflows and undermines customer trust in the status page. This PR implements anti-flap windows and hysteresis in the status-signal evaluator so we only transition component state when a condition persists long enough, and we require a healthier condition to recover than the one that caused the degradation. Summary of changes: Adds a small state machine around each status signal (errors + latency) with configurable (1) trigger window (min duration / min samples) and (2) recovery window (min duration / min samples) plus (3) hysteresis thresholds (degrade_threshold vs recover_threshold). Also adds per-component overrides and emits evaluator metrics so we can audit how often anti-flap prevented a transition. Behavior details: Degrade requires threshold breach for N consecutive evaluations (default 3 of 3 at 1m cadence) OR for a minimum time window (default 5m) depending on signal type; recover requires the metric to be back under a stricter recovery threshold for a longer window (default 10m). A note on semantics: this is intentionally separate from SLO burn alerts; we’re smoothing the customer-facing component health classification, not masking genuine outages. Rollout plan: guarded behind status_signals.anti_flap_enabled (default off). In dry-run mode we compute both old/new state and publish a diff metric (status_signals_state_diff_total{component_id,signal_type}) to validate no surprises. After 1 week of dry-run we’ll enable for Hosted API components in us-east + eu-west, then expand. Docs: Updated README in the pack and added inline docs for config knobs and default values. Testing: Adds unit tests for threshold crossing, jitter, and recovery hysteresis; adds a regression test that reproduces the us-east streaming ‘toggle’ pattern from early Nov. Related: Status page transparency upgrades project; aligns with internal standard for customer-facing status signals (anti-flap guidance + thresholds).

title: Support approval step for Sev0/Sev1 status page publishes (optional)
preview:
Context: As part of the Status page transparency upgrades initiative, we’ve standardized comms templates and added Comms Lead reminders. One gap that came up repeatedly (Support escalations + game days) is that during Sev0/Sev1 incidents we sometimes publish status updates with wording that Support has to immediately clarify, or we publish a component impact that later turns out to be wrong (especially when impact is limited to a subset of customers/regions). This PR introduces an optional approval step in incident-bot before publishing status page updates for Sev0/Sev1, allowing either (a) the on-call Incident Commander or (b) an on-call Support lead to approve the draft. This is intended to improve accuracy without adding overhead to Sev2+ events.

What’s changing:
- Adds a configurable “publish requires approval” gate that activates only for Sev0/Sev1 incidents and only when the action is “publish to Statuspage” (drafting continues to work without approval).
- Approver can be one of:
  - Incident Commander (IC) (default)
  - Support Lead (optional, enabled via config per environment)
- Approval is time-bound: if no approval within N minutes, bot posts a reminder in #incidents and DM’s the Comms Lead + IC. (Does not auto-publish.)
- Adds an explicit “urgent publish” override for IC only (requires a typed confirmation string) for cases where status page time-to-first-update is at risk and Support is unavailable.
- Audit logs: records who approved, when, and the exact message payload + components/regions at publish time (for later accuracy audits).

Key behavior details:
- Sev2+ incidents: no approval gate; behavior unchanged.
- Sev0/Sev1 incidents:
  - /statuspage draft works as today and posts the draft to the incident channel.
  - /statuspage publish triggers approval gate unless config disables it.
  - Approval can happen via Slack button (“Approve publish”) or slash command (/incident approve statuspage).
  - If Support approval is enabled and Support Lead is assigned, Support Lead can approve; otherwise IC approval is sufficient.
- If the incident has no statuspage components selected, publish is blocked with a clearer error pointing to the component fields (ties into the earlier component validation work).

Why optional (and not mandatory): Not all incidents have active Support coverage in the first minutes, and we don’t want to regress TTFU for Sev0/Sev1. The gate can be enabled per workspace/environment. For now we’ll enable in prod for Sev0/Sev1 only, with the IC urgent override available.

Implementation notes:
- Introduces a small “ApprovalGate” interface with a StatuspagePublishGate implementation.
- Slack interactivity: new block actions for approve/deny; deny requires a short reason which is posted back into the incident channel thread.
- Permissions: only users in roles {incident_commander, support_lead} can approve. Role mapping uses the existing incident roles source (no new directory/service).
- Config:
  - statuspage.publish_approval.enabled: true|false
  - statuspage.publish_approval.severities: ["sev0","sev1"]
  - statuspage.publish_approval.approvers: ["ic","support_lead"]
  - statuspage.publish_approval.reminder_minutes: integer

Changed areas/files (high level):
- src/workflows/statuspage/publish.ts: wrap publish action with approval gate
- src/workflows/approvals/gates/statuspage_publish_gate.ts: new gate
- src/slack/blocks/statuspage_publish_approval.ts: Slack UI blocks + actions
- src/slack/handlers/actions.ts: wire approve/deny actions
- src/audit/audit_log.ts: append approval metadata fields
- config/schema.ts + config/defaults.ts: new config entries
- docs/runbooks/statuspage.md: operator guidance for Comms Lead + IC
- tests/workflows/statuspage_publish_approval.test.ts: new coverage for gating + override

Screenshots/UX:
- Added a minimal approval card in the incident channel thread with buttons: Approve publish / Deny (reason required) / IC urgent override.

Rollout plan:
1) Deploy behind config flag off in staging.
2) Enable in staging for Sev0/Sev1 and run through game-day checklist.
3) Enable in prod for Sev0/Sev1 with approvers=[ic,support_lead] and reminder_minutes=5.
4) After 2 weeks, review metrics: time-to-first-update, number of denied publishes, and support escalations about inaccurate status updates.

Risk/mitigations:
- Risk: delays publishing. Mitigation: gate only Sev0/Sev1, reminders at 5m, IC urgent override.
- Risk: role assignment wrong -> no approver. Mitigation: falls back to IC if Support Lead not present; explicit error includes command to assign roles.

Release notes:
- incident-bot: Optional approval step for status page publish actions during Sev0/Sev1 incidents; adds audit logging of approver + timestamps.

Refs:
- Linear: ENG-4860 (approval workflow), ENG-4822 (statuspage integration)
- Jira: INT-10430 (support macros alignment), SUP-28436 (disputed accuracy during US-East degradation)

Checklist:
- [x] Unit tests for approval gate + override
- [x] Updated runbook docs
- [x] Verified no behavior change for Sev2+
- [x] Added audit log fields and verified in staging

title: Link SLO burn alerts to status page update runbook + component taxonomy
preview: Context: As part of the status page transparency upgrades, on-call needs a fast, consistent path from a page to (a) the correct status-page component taxonomy and (b) the exact runbook steps for posting/updating component statuses. Today, most of our burn-rate alerts include a generic runbook link (or no runbook at all), and nothing points to the canonical taxonomy/mapping guidance. This PR updates alert annotations across component-level SLO burn alerts in this repo to include: (1) a status page update runbook link, and (2) a status page component taxonomy link. It also standardizes annotation keys so incident-bot and human responders can reliably surface the right links in Slack. Scope: alert rule annotations + lint/test updates only; no threshold or routing changes. Notes: we intentionally keep the links to high-level canonical docs rather than incident-specific pages, and we avoid language that implies an SLA/guarantee. Rollout: safe to merge; annotations-only; validated in staging alertmanager and via jsonnet render output. Follow-ups: once merged, we can have incident-bot surface these fields automatically in the intake + reminders (tracked elsewhere).

title: Add KMS throttle budget + request backpressure to prevent KMS saturation under spikes
preview:
Context: We’ve seen intermittent elevated 5xx correlated with upstream KMS throttling (SUP-19377) and a near-miss retry amplification event that required a hotfix (pr-18563). While the wrapper now has sane retry/jitter defaults and circuit breaking, we still had a failure mode where a large burst of decrypt/envelope ops (e.g. cache cold start + traffic spike) could saturate KMS and extend the incident blast radius.

This PR introduces an explicit "KMS throttle budget" and integrates KMS calls with the existing request admission/backpressure path so we shed/queue work earlier rather than stampeding KMS.

What’s included (high level): (1) Per-process token-bucket budget for KMS operations: A lightweight budget that is decremented on KMS requests and refilled at a configured rate. When depleted, KMS calls are delayed (bounded) or rejected with a typed error depending on caller policy. This keeps concurrency and request rate within a predictable envelope even if upstream is degraded. (2) Adaptive refill + penalty on throttling: When KMS returns throttling (e.g., AWS ThrottlingException / 429 equivalents), we apply an immediate penalty to the budget refill rate for a short window. This quickly reduces pressure and avoids repeated throttles. (3) Backpressure integration: The KMS client wrapper now plugs into `admission.Controller` so callers can either: (a) block up to `kms.backpressure.max_wait_ms` and then fail fast, or (b) fail immediately (for latency-sensitive paths) with a clear error category (`KmsBudgetExhausted`). (4) Metrics + tracing: Exposes budget state and decisions, and tags spans with the backpressure outcome.

Config knobs (all have safe defaults; prod guardrails still enforced by pr-18502): - `kms.budget.refill_rps` (default: 200 ops/sec) - `kms.budget.burst` (default: 400 ops) - `kms.backpressure.max_wait_ms` (default: 50ms) - `kms.budget.throttle_penalty_window_ms` (default: 30000ms) - `kms.budget.throttle_penalty_factor` (default: 0.5)

Behavioral notes: - This budget is per-process (not global) by design. It limits local amplification and works well with our horizontal scaling/admission control. If we need global limits later, we can layer it via control plane config + per-pool budgets. - We do NOT silently drop decrypts. If we can’t meet the wait bound, we return a typed error and let the caller’s policy decide whether to fail the request, fall back, or rely on cached material (where allowed). - When combined with the circuit breaker, the system now transitions more cleanly: budget starts slowing requests first; if KMS remains degraded, the breaker opens and callers follow degrade-mode policy.

Implementation details: - `kmsbudget.Bucket`: token-bucket with penalty hooks; uses monotonic clock; jittered sleeps to avoid herd effects at refill boundaries. - `kmsclient.Client`: wraps `Encrypt/Decrypt/GenerateDataKey` calls with `Acquire(ctx)`; all outcomes emit structured logs and metrics. - `admission.Controller`: added a dependency-specific limiter hook (KMS only for now) so we can reuse the backpressure path without leaking KMS specifics into product code.

Metrics added: - `redwood_kms_budget_tokens{env,region,service}` (gauge) - `redwood_kms_budget_acquire_total{outcome="immediate|waited|timeout|rejected"}` (counter) - `redwood_kms_budget_wait_ms` (histogram) - `redwood_kms_throttle_penalty_active` (gauge) - Existing `redwood_kms_errors_total{category=...}` is now incremented for `BudgetExhausted` and `BackpressureTimeout` (maps to the canonical taxonomy).

Example log fields (new/standardized): `kms_op`, `kms_budget_outcome`, `kms_budget_wait_ms`, `kms_budget_tokens_after`, `kms_error_category`, `trace_id`.

Testing: - Unit tests for token bucket correctness and penalty behavior (including time skews). - Integration harness updated to simulate 429/throttling and verify that request rate to the KMS stub decreases within 2s and that tail latency stays bounded (ties into ENG-4825). - Added a regression test ensuring we don’t exceed retry+budget wait combined latency cap.

Rollout plan: - Behind `kms.backpressure.enabled` (default ON in staging, OFF in prod until we flip per environment). - Canary in us-east Dedicated + Hosted data-plane: monitor `redwood_kms_budget_wait_ms p95`, KMS 429 rate, and request 5xx. - Flip prod flag gradually per region; rollback is a single flag change.

Checklist: - [x] SRE reviewed paging/threshold implications (no new pages by default; dashboards use derived panels) - [x] Security reviewed failure semantics (no weakening of envelope decrypt correctness) - [x] Added metrics + trace tags per observability conventions - [x] Added tests for throttle + backpressure scenarios

Follow-ups: - Consider exposing recommended budget defaults in the Private operator guide once we validate prod behavior across 2+ regions. - Evaluate whether control plane should push pool-specific budgets for Dedicated customers with known KMS quotas.

Related: ENG-4821, ENG-4825, ENG-4828; incident context SUP-19377; complements pr-18563 (retry storm hotfix), pr-18449 (circuit breaker), pr-18502 (config guardrails).

title: Statuspage API client: retry/backoff + idempotency keys + audit log entries
preview: Problem: incident-bot’s Statuspage integration currently performs best-effort POST/PATCH calls with minimal retry handling. During Sev0/Sev1s we’ve seen transient 5xx/timeout/429 responses (plus occasional network blips) leading to (a) failed publishes that require manual retry and (b) duplicate updates when a human re-triggers a step while the original request is still in flight. This PR introduces a small Statuspage API client wrapper with standardized retry/backoff, idempotency keys, and structured audit log entries for every attempted publish/update. Scope: add an HTTP client with (1) bounded retries for retryable failures (429, 5xx, network timeouts), (2) exponential backoff with jitter and respect for Retry-After where present, (3) deterministic idempotency keys so retries and replays don’t create duplicate incidents/updates, and (4) audit logging to our internal incident-bot audit sink so we can answer “what did we publish, when, and who/what triggered it?” without scraping Slack. Non-goals: changing the copy/templates (handled in PR-19344) or expanding component taxonomy fields (handled in PR-19321). Implementation details: The new StatuspageClient wraps fetch with a retry policy: max_attempts=5 (1 initial + 4 retries), base_delay_ms=250, max_delay_ms=5000, full jitter. We retry on: HTTP 429, HTTP 500/502/503/504, and network errors/timeouts. We do NOT retry on other 4xx. For 429 we honor Retry-After (seconds) if present, otherwise use computed backoff. Idempotency: we add an Idempotency-Key header on requests that create resources (incident create, incident update create). Key format is stable and derived from: statuspage_page_id, incident_id (our internal incident GUID), operation (create_incident | post_update | set_component_status), component_ids (sorted), and a normalized “update_fingerprint” (state + message hash + impact window start rounded to minute). This keeps keys stable across retries but different across meaningfully different updates. Audit log: every call to StatuspageClient emits an AuditEvent with request_id, operation, page_id, statuspage_incident_id (if known), idempotency_key, http_status, attempt_count, elapsed_ms, and redacted payload summary (we keep message length + sha256, not the full message). Audit events go to the existing incident-bot audit stream (and show up in the internal dashboard), and we also log a one-line summary to #incidents-bot-debug on failures after max attempts. Testing: added unit tests for retry classification, backoff calculations (including Retry-After), idempotency key stability, and audit event emission; added one integration-ish test that stubs the Statuspage API with a 429 then a 200 to validate behavior. Ops notes: config defaults live in statuspage_client.yaml but can be overridden via env vars for emergency tuning (MAX_ATTEMPTS, MAX_DELAY_MS).

Key format is stable and derived from: statuspage_page_id, incident_id (our internal incident GUID), operation (create_incident | post_update | set_component_status), component_ids (sorted), and a normalized “update_fingerprint” (state + message hash + impact window start rounded to minute). This keeps keys stable across retries but different across meaningfully different updates. Audit log: every call to StatuspageClient emits an AuditEvent with request_id, operation, page_id, statuspage_incident_id (if known), idempotency_key, http_status, attempt_count, elapsed_ms, and redacted payload summary (we keep message length + sha256, not the full message). Audit events go to the existing incident-bot audit stream (and show up in the internal dashboard), and we also log a one-line summary to #incidents-bot-debug on failures after max attempts. Testing: added unit tests for retry classification, backoff calculations (including Retry-After), idempotency key stability, and audit event emission; added one integration-ish test that stubs the Statuspage API with a 429 then a 200 to validate behavior. Ops notes: config defaults live in statuspage_client.yaml but can be overridden via env vars for emergency tuning (MAX_ATTEMPTS, MAX_DELAY_MS).

title: Add overload shedding dashboards + initial alert rules (breaker opens, shed rate)
preview: Context: As part of the Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding reliability program, we need first-class visibility into (a) when we are shedding traffic vs failing implicitly, and (b) when breakers are opening/half-opening/closing across regions/models/tiers. This PR adds Grafana dashboards and initial Prometheus alert rules for overload protection signals. Scope: (1) Dashboards: Add "Hosted API • Overload Protection" dashboard group with panels for shed rate, breaker open rate, admission decisions, runtime saturation proxies (queue depth / token saturation / memory pressure proxy), and top shed reasons. Includes recommended drilldowns by region and route family, and a safe set of labels (bounded cardinality). (2) Alerts: Add first-pass paging + ticket alerts for (i) sustained breaker-open rate, (ii) elevated shed rate for paid tiers, (iii) Retry-After header missing on overload responses (via gateway metric), and (iv) mismatch between shed and runtime saturation (possible false positive / bad thresholds). Each alert includes runbook links and suggested next actions. (3) Runbook links: Links to oncall runbook and runtime mitigation guide; includes incident-bot channel references. Notes/guardrails: We intentionally avoid tenant_id and request_id as dimensions. Model is aggregated to a small allowlist via label mapping in the gateway metrics exporter. Dashboards default to last 1h and show p95/p99 lat overlay to correlate shedding with tail latency improvements. Follow-ups: (a) tighten alert thresholds after canary (false positives), (b) add per-tier SLO burn overlays once SLO toolkit classification is rolled out everywhere, (c) add a "top fallback attempts" panel after routing PR-18458 is fully deployed. Testing: `make lint`, `make test`, `make dashboards-validate`, `make prom-rules-test`.

title: Smart Routing: fallback model/region when overload breaker is open (bounded + loop-safe)
preview:
Context: As part of the hosted overload protection work, we want to degrade gracefully instead of cascading timeouts when a model/region is saturated. This PR adds a routing-side fallback attempt when the upstream admission layer reports an overload breaker open (or equivalent overload classification).

What this PR does: Implements a fallback resolution step in Smart Routing that can (optionally) route to a compatible fallback model and/or fallback region based on tenant + route policy. Adds strict loop prevention so we don’t bounce between model variants/regions, and bounds fallback attempts to a small fixed number to avoid amplifying load. Also ensures we do not fallback for non-overload failures (e.g., auth, invalid params, quota exceeded).

Key behaviors: (1) Trigger conditions: only when overload classification indicates breaker open / load shed / capacity unavailable (see mapping in code comments); not triggered on generic 5xx. (2) Fallback selection: consults policy engine for allowed fallbacks for the tenant + route + requested model; if none configured, behave as before. (3) Bounded attempts: max 1 fallback per request by default (configurable to 2 for region+model hop, but guarded); stops immediately if fallback target equals original target. (4) Loop prevention: propagates an internal routing context header across internal hops and records visited (model, region) pairs; if the candidate is already visited, we do not attempt it. (5) Observability: emits counters for fallback_attempted, fallback_succeeded, fallback_blocked_loop, and fallback_exhausted; dimensions intentionally low-cardinality (tenant_tier, route_group, requested_model_family, result).

Why routing (not gateway): The gateway admission layer is responsible for shedding early, but only routing knows compatibility constraints and tenant policy for fallback targets. This is consistent with the admission layering guidance (see ADR-015) — the gateway classifies overload; routing decides a safe alternate target; runtime remains the source of saturation signals.

Safety constraints: Dedicated and Private tenants are never routed to Hosted. Hosted self-serve can fallback within Hosted only. Enterprise Hosted can fallback only to configured allowlisted model variants and regions. We also explicitly disallow cross-AZ fallback within a region to avoid hiding infra issues; region fallback is an explicit opt-in.

Testing: Unit tests cover loop prevention, bounded retries, policy deny, and correct trigger mapping. Integration test fakes breaker-open signals and verifies we attempt exactly one fallback and preserve the correlation ID. Perf-canary scenario work is tracked separately.

Follow-ups: (a) tune default max attempts and error mapping once we have canary data; (b) add dashboard panel for fallback rates vs shed rate; (c) document customer-facing behavior once GA’d (docs owned by DevEx/Docs).

title: Generate Statuspage update drafts from incident state (phase templates + metrics)
preview:
Context: As part of the status page transparency upgrades, we want to reduce time-to-first-update and improve consistency across incident phases without increasing on-call burden. This PR adds a draft generator that produces templated Statuspage update text based on the current incident state (Investigating/Identified/Mitigating/Monitoring/Resolved). Drafts pull in the structured fields we already collect at intake (component/region/capability) plus optional impact metrics and known mitigations. The output is a DRAFT only (explicitly labeled) and requires a human publish step.

What this does: Adds a template registry for each incident phase with a small variable set (component_display, regions, capabilities, started_at, current_state, impact_summary, error_rate, p95_latency_ms, affected_routes, customer_visible_symptoms, mitigations_in_progress, fallback_active, next_update_eta). Adds a DraftStatusUpdate action to the incident-bot workflow that (1) reads incident state, (2) computes an impact summary string, (3) renders the appropriate template, and (4) posts the draft to #incidents and stores it on the incident record for copy/paste or for the Statuspage API client step in a follow-up PR. If metrics are missing, the template degrades gracefully (no "N/A" leakage; it omits the line). Includes tone guardrails: do not mention specific customers, do not imply SLA credits, do not speculate root cause, and keep updates < 900 chars for Statuspage readability.

Template examples (rendered): Investigating: "We are investigating reports of elevated errors for Hosted API > Chat Completions in us-east. Some requests may fail or see increased latency. Next update in 30 minutes." Identified: "We identified an issue impacting Streaming responses in eu-west. We are working on mitigation and will provide updates as progress is made." Mitigating: "Mitigation is in progress (traffic shifting + capacity rebalance). Impact is improving but some errors may persist." Monitoring: "Mitigation has been applied and we are monitoring recovery metrics." Resolved: "This incident has been resolved. A post-incident summary will be published when available."

Implementation notes: Templates live in src/comms/templates/statuspage.ts and are keyed by IncidentPhase. We added a lightweight renderer (no external templating deps) to keep the bot footprint small and deterministic. Metric selection is intentionally conservative: error rate is preferred; latency-only degradations are included only when explicitly marked in incident state (avoids false positives). We also include an optional "Next update" line; default is 30m for Sev0/Sev1, 60m otherwise, but can be overridden.

Testing: Unit tests cover template rendering, omission rules, and safety checks (no customer names / ticket IDs). Added snapshot tests for each phase with representative incident payloads (Hosted API, Streaming, Embeddings). Manual test instructions included in docs/runbooks/statuspage-drafts.md.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): wiring to the Statuspage API client wrapper with audit logs (PR-19405) and adding an approval step for Sev0/Sev1 publish actions (PR-19433).

Screenshots/logs: Included in the conversation thread as bot output in a test incident (see "Draft generated" comment).

title: Announce circuit breaker state transitions to #incidents and #eng-oncall
preview:
Context
As part of the Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding program (ENG-4821), we need a low-latency, human-visible signal when breakers start shedding (OPEN) and when they recover (HALF_OPEN/CLOSED). The current workflow relies on dashboards/alerts only; during incidents this increases time-to-diagnosis and contributes to duplicate triage.

This PR teaches incident-bot to announce breaker state transitions into Slack (#incidents and #eng-oncall) with:
- Transition summary (CLOSED → OPEN, OPEN → HALF_OPEN, HALF_OPEN → CLOSED)
- Scope (region, route, model selector, tenant/tier when available)
- Top contributing signal(s) (queue depth, token/sec saturation, dependency health)
- Links to dashboards and the oncall runbook
- A short “recommended actions” block aligned with the runbook

Behavior
- Posts OPEN transitions to both #incidents and #eng-oncall.
- Posts recovery transitions (OPEN→HALF_OPEN, HALF_OPEN→CLOSED) to #eng-oncall only by default to reduce noise.
- Dedupes repeated OPEN transitions for the same breaker_key within a configurable window (default 10m).
- If event payload does not include route/model details, message still posts with breaker_key + region and a note about missing dimensions.

Implementation details
- Adds a new event consumer for `overload.breaker_state_changed` from the internal event bus (same transport as alert notifications).
- Introduces `BreakerKey` normalization to keep dedupe stable (region + route_group + model_selector + tier + policy_id).
- Adds Slack message formatting using Block Kit, including:
  - severity coloring (OPEN = danger, HALF_OPEN = warning, CLOSED = good)
  - buttons: “Open dashboard”, “Open runbook”, “Search logs”
- Uses `dashboards.redwood.ai` and `runbooks.redwood.ai` deep links with query params prefilled (region, time range last 30m, breaker_key).

Recommended actions (message content)
- OPEN: Verify whether this is capacity saturation vs downstream brownout; check shed-rate + runtime queue depth; confirm Retry-After is being set; page routing owner if fallback loops suspected.
- HALF_OPEN: Watch error budget burn + shed rate; do not raise caps until p95 stabilizes.
- CLOSED: Capture incident timestamp window; confirm alerts cleared; annotate postmortem with breaker_key and top signals.

Safety/guardrails
- Hard cap of 1 Slack message per breaker_key per channel per 10 minutes (configurable).
- Suppresses messages for canary-only breakers unless `announce_canary=true` is set (default false).
- Sanitizes tenant IDs: only posts tenant slug if explicitly marked as `shareable=true` in the event payload.

Testing
- Unit tests for normalization, dedupe window, channel routing, and link generation.
- Integration-style test with a mocked Slack client to validate Block Kit payload.

Rollout
- Deploy behind flag `INCIDENT_BOT_BREAKER_ANNOUNCEMENTS=enabled`.
- Start enabled in staging; prod enablement to follow canary rollout go/no-go email thread.

Screenshots (example Slack message)
[OPEN] Hosted breaker opened: us-east / chat.completions / model=rw-llama-3-70b (tier=self-serve)
Signals: runtime.queue_depth_p95=182, token_saturation=0.93
Shed rate: 14% (last 5m)
Actions: Check Overload Protection dashboard; consider temporary max_tokens cap policy; verify Retry-After present; evaluate fallback policy health
Buttons: Open dashboard | Open runbook | Search logs

Notes
- This PR does not change alerting rules; it is a complementary “human paging context” path.
- Follow-up: thread-safe in-memory dedupe should move to Redis if we run multiple incident-bot replicas (tracked in ENG-4869).

title: Add component-level SLO dashboards + standard links for status page components
preview: Context: As part of the Status Page Transparency Upgrades initiative, on-call needs a consistent, component-level view of latency + error impact that matches the public status component taxonomy (component_id x region x capability). Today dashboards are a mix of service names (gateway/scheduler/streamer), inconsistent region labels, and ad-hoc links in runbooks. This PR adds a standardized dashboard set per status component and a link registry so alerts/runbooks/status tooling can point to the same canonical URLs. What’s in this PR: (1) New Grafana dashboards for each public status component, split into latency + errors with consistent panels and templating. (2) Standard naming conventions for dashboards, folders, variables, and panel titles (to reduce “is this the right chart?” time during Sev1). (3) A generated link map (component_id/region/capability -> dashboard URL) committed under observability-pack so it can be referenced from alert annotations and the status-page update runbook. (4) Basic validation to ensure every active component in the registry has a dashboard link entry (fails CI if missing). Scope/Components: Hosted API (chat/completions), Streaming, Embeddings, Reranking, Console (incident surfacing + status endpoints), plus region views for us-east-1, us-west-2, eu-west-1. Notes: Dedicated/Private are intentionally excluded from the public-facing component dashboard set in this PR; we can add internal-only dashboards later under a separate folder with restricted perms. Naming convention: Folder = "Status Components"; Dashboard title = "Status Component: <Display Name> (<region>)"; UID = "statuscomp-<component_id>-<region>"; Variables include component_id, region, capability (where applicable), route_group. Panels standardize on p50/p95/p99 latency, error rate (5m/30m), request volume, saturation proxy (queue depth where available), and top routes contributing to errors. Validation/rollout: This is safe to merge behind the current component registry; new components will require link entries, but the check only enforces for components marked rollout_enabled=true. Follow-ups: The anti-flap logic PR will reference these dashboards in alert annotations; the status-page update runbook will also be updated to prefer the canonical links from this repo.

title: Add JSON schema + CI validation for status component registry
preview: This PR introduces a JSON Schema for the public status-page component registry and adds validation in CI so registry changes fail fast when they are malformed or violate taxonomy rules. Motivation: we’re using the registry as a source-of-truth for component ids/display names/ownership and rollout flags as part of the Status page transparency upgrades effort; recent incidents and hotfixes showed that small typos (ids, missing fields, inconsistent region/capability labels) can cause misclassification and customer-facing status inaccuracies. What’s included: (1) New schema file `statuspage/component-registry.schema.json` that defines required fields and constraints for each component entry: `component_id` (kebab-case, stable, unique), `display_name` (human readable), `product_area` (hosted_api|streaming|embeddings|reranking|console|dedicated_control_plane), `regions` (allowed set), `capabilities` (allowed set), `ownership` (team + slack channel), and `rollout` flags (`public_enabled`, `console_link_enabled`, `auto_updates_enabled`) with defaults. (2) Validation script `scripts/validate-status-component-registry.ts` (AJV) that validates `statuspage/component-registry.json` against the schema and additionally enforces a few cross-field rules that JSON Schema can’t express cleanly (e.g., no duplicate display_name within the same product_area+region group, `public_enabled=false` components must not have `console_link_enabled=true`, and region list must be non-empty for region-scoped product areas). (3) CI wiring: adds a `statuspage-registry-validate` job to the existing checks and runs it on PRs that touch `statuspage/**`. (4) Developer ergonomics: `npm run validate:statuspage-registry` command, plus README updates describing how to add a new component safely and how to handle deprecated/renamed ids (no renames; add new id and mark old id `public_enabled=false` only after deprecation window). Notes: This does not change runtime/statuspage behavior directly; it only adds guardrails for future registry edits. Follow-ups will update incident-bot + gateway metrics tagging to rely on the validated ids (tracked in ENG-4822/ENG-4830).
text: This PR introduces a JSON Schema for the public status-page component registry and adds validation in CI so registry changes fail fast when they are malformed or violate taxonomy rules. Motivation: we’re using the registry as a source-of-truth for component ids/display names/ownership and rollout flags as part of the Status page transparency upgrades effort; recent incidents and hotfixes showed that small typos (ids, missing fields, inconsistent region/capability labels) can cause misclassification and customer-facing status inaccuracies. What’s included: (1) New schema file `statuspage/component-registry.schema.json` that defines required fields and constraints for each component entry: `component_id` (kebab-case, stable, unique), `display_name` (human readable), `product_area` (hosted_api|streaming|embeddings|reranking|console|dedicated_control_plane), `regions` (allowed set), `capabilities` (allowed set), `ownership` (team + slack channel), and `rollout` flags (`public_enabled`, `console_link_enabled`, `auto_updates_enabled`) with defaults. (2) Validation script `scripts/validate-status-component-registry.ts` (AJV) that validates `statuspage/component-registry.json` against the schema and additionally enforces a few cross-field rules that JSON Schema can’t express cleanly (e.g., no duplicate display_name within the same product_area+region group, `public_enabled=false` components must not have `console_link_enabled=true`, and region list must be non-empty for region-scoped product areas). (3) CI wiring: adds a `statuspage-registry-validate` job to the existing checks and runs it on PRs that touch `statuspage/**`. (4) Developer ergonomics: `npm run validate:statuspage-registry` command, plus README updates describing how to add a new component safely and how to handle deprecated/renamed ids (no renames; add new id and mark old id `public_enabled=false` only after deprecation window). Notes: This does not change runtime/statuspage behavior directly; it only adds guardrails for future registry edits. Follow-ups will update incident-bot + gateway metrics tagging to rely on the validated ids (tracked in ENG-4822/ENG-4830).

title: gateway: add admission control middleware (token bucket + concurrency caps) wired to overload policy engine
preview:
Title: gateway: add admission control middleware (token bucket + concurrency caps) wired to overload policy engine

Context
We’re starting Phase 1 for Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding (ENG-4821). A recurring failure mode in incidents is the gateway allowing bursts to fully propagate to routing/runtime, which creates tail-latency blowups, timeouts, and retry storms.

This PR adds a first admission-control layer in the API gateway with:
- Token bucket rate limiting (RPS) keyed by tenant + route (+ optional model selector)
- Concurrency caps (in-flight requests) keyed by tenant + route (with separate buckets for streaming vs non-streaming)
- Policy evaluation via the overload policy engine (tenant overrides + tier defaults), including a safe-mode fallback when the policy engine is unavailable
- Metrics for “allowed vs rejected” and limiter state (designed to keep cardinality bounded)

This is intended to work in concert with the overload error taxonomy (ADR-014 / PR #18421) but does not change payload semantics beyond calling the shared error helper.

What’s included
1) Middleware: AdmissionControl
- New middleware inserted after auth/tenant resolution and before routing.
- Enforces two independent limiters:
  a) TokenBucketLimiter: per (tenant_id, route_id, traffic_class) with optional model_id selector when configured
  b) ConcurrencyLimiter: per (tenant_id, route_id, traffic_class)
- “traffic_class” is one of: standard, streaming. This is to avoid streaming consuming all inflight slots.

2) Policy engine integration
- Pulls effective policy from policy engine (tier defaults + tenant overrides) with a small local cache.
- Supports enforcement modes:
  - disabled: middleware is a no-op
  - dry_run: evaluates + emits metrics but never rejects
  - enforce: rejects when limits exceeded
- Safety behavior when policy engine errors/timeouts:
  - Default: fail-open (allow) + metric redwood_gateway_admission_policy_fetch_errors_total
  - Optional per-region flag fail_closed_for_tiers=[enterprise-hosted] (kept off for now; follow-up PR will add rollout controls).

3) Errors
- Uses shared overload error helper to return stable, customer-facing semantics:
  - HTTP 429 when rate limited
  - HTTP 429 when concurrency limited (this matches current guidance for “client should retry with backoff”; we can revisit mapping as ADR-014 evolves)
- Adds a new internal reason code passed through the helper:
  - reason=GATEWAY_RATE_LIMIT
  - reason=GATEWAY_CONCURRENCY_LIMIT
NOTE: Retry-After consistency is handled in PR #18472; this PR only sets it opportunistically when the policy provides a duration.

4) Metrics (Prometheus)
All metrics are emitted with bounded labels only:
- redwood_gateway_admission_requests_total{result="allowed|rejected", limiter="token_bucket|concurrency", route_id, tier}
- redwood_gateway_admission_rejections_total{reason="rate|concurrency", route_id, tier}
- redwood_gateway_admission_inflight{route_id, tier, traffic_class}
- redwood_gateway_admission_token_bucket_remaining{route_id, tier} (gauge sampled at a fixed interval; no per-tenant label)
- redwood_gateway_admission_policy_fetch_latency_ms_bucket{tier}
- redwood_gateway_admission_policy_fetch_errors_total{tier}

We explicitly do NOT label by tenant_id to avoid unbounded series; tenant-level debugging is via request logs + correlation IDs.

Implementation notes
- Token bucket is implemented as a local in-process limiter with periodic refill. This is intentionally approximate at first; it still reduces overload cascades by applying backpressure early. We rely on existing gateway sharding to keep per-tenant skew manageable. Follow-ups can add distributed enforcement for specific tiers.
- Concurrency limiter uses an atomic in-flight counter per key with a defer-based release to ensure counters decrement on all code paths (including streaming cancel).
- Streaming requests acquire concurrency slots at request start; they release on stream close or client cancel.

Changed areas (high-level)
- gateway/middleware/admission_control.go: new middleware + wiring
- gateway/limiters/token_bucket.go: token bucket limiter implementation
- gateway/limiters/concurrency.go: in-flight counter limiter
- gateway/policy/overload_policy_client.go: policy fetch + caching + enforcement mode
- gateway/errors/overload.go: reason mappings (shared helper)
- gateway/observability/metrics.go: new metrics registration
- gateway/config/flags.go: feature flags for enforcement mode + cache TTL
- gateway/tests/admission_control_test.go: unit tests (allowed/rejected, streaming release, policy failures)

Illustrative snippet (middleware wiring)
- Added AdmissionControl after TenantContext and before Router:
  - chain: Auth -> TenantContext -> AdmissionControl -> Router -> ResponseWriter

Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - Token bucket rejects after N requests within refill window
  - Concurrency rejects when inflight > cap
  - Streaming cancellation releases inflight slot
  - Policy engine timeout -> fail-open behavior + metric
- Manual: ran local gateway against perf-canary replay; verified dry-run emits metrics without customer impact.

Rollout plan
- Default mode is disabled in config.
- For canary region, we will enable dry_run for self-serve tier on /v1/chat.completions and /v1/embeddings first to gather baseline.
- After thresholds tuned (tuning sheet), we will move to enforce for a small tenant allowlist.

Links
- Linear: ENG-4823 (this PR), ENG-4821 (program)
- Related PRs: #18421 (error codes/payload), #18441 (policy schema/validation), #18472 (Retry-After + client guidance)
- Support signal: SUP-2860 (Retry-After missing on some overload paths; addressed separately but referenced here)

Commits
1. gateway: introduce admission control middleware skeleton
2. gateway: add token bucket limiter + tests
3. gateway: add concurrency limiter (streaming-safe) + tests
4. gateway: integrate overload policy engine client + caching
5. gateway: metrics for admission decisions + policy fetch
6. gateway: wire middleware into request chain (behind flag)
7. gateway: address review feedback (cardinality, fail-open behavior, docs)

Review conversation (highlights)
- Ethan Park (requested changes): “Make sure we don’t create a new per-tenant metric series explosion. Also clarify where in the chain this runs (before routing) and add notes on behavior when policy engine is down.”
  - Amara Diallo: Updated metric labels to remove tenant_id, added bounded route_id/tier only; added explicit fail-open behavior and a metric for policy fetch errors; documented middleware order.
- Benji Okafor (requested changes): “Policy schema allows model selectors; confirm how you handle route+model conflicts. Also please ensure default mode is dry_run/disabled (not enforce).”
  - Amara Diallo: Implemented selector precedence: tenant override > tier default; route-only rules apply if model selector absent; confirmed default disabled.
- Christine Wong (comment): “Concurrency limiter must release on streaming early close + context cancel. Please add a regression test.”
  - Amara Diallo: Added streaming cancellation test + ensured release happens in stream finalizer.
- Rafael Mendes (requested changes): “Fail-open is OK for now, but add a clear counter + alert hook; also add a short note about how this interacts with retry storms.”
  - Amara Diallo: Added redwood_gateway_admission_policy_fetch_errors_total; added doc note that we should pair enforcement with consistent Retry-After (PR #18472) to reduce retry storms.
- Kaitlyn Nguyen (approve): “Looks good; auth/tenant resolution happens before admission, so limits are correctly keyed. Thanks for keeping it after auth.”

CI
- gateway-unit-tests: PASS
- gateway-integration-tests: PASS (1st run flaky due to unrelated redis container startup; rerun PASS)
- lint: PASS
- staticcheck: PASS

Merge
- Merged via squash into main.
- Follow-ups:
  - Add strict Retry-After and client guidance enforcement (#18472)
  - Add canary config bundles per region/tier once policy schema PR lands (#18441)
  - Add dashboard panels for these new metrics (observability-pack PR #913)

title: hotfix(kms): reduce retry amplification on timeouts + add jitter
preview:
Context: We had a near-miss on 2025-11-16 where transient KMS timeouts caused a retry storm and elevated 5xx due to retry amplification across layers (gateway -> control-plane client -> kms wrapper). This PR is a production hotfix to reduce amplification, introduce jitter, and ensure we respect an end-to-end retry budget so we don’t turn a dependency blip into a load event. Incident thread: slack://incidents/1747779033-near-miss-kms-retry-storm (related earlier spike: slack://incidents/1746508855-kms-timeout-spike-us-east). Customer correlation: SUP-19377.

What changed (high level): 1) Enforce a single retry budget per request for KMS decrypt/envelope operations (attempts + time). 2) Switch timeout retry backoff to exponential w/ full jitter (AWS recommended) and cap max sleep. 3) Stop retrying once the call is past the request deadline (previously we could start a new attempt with <50ms remaining). 4) Add guardrail to prevent nested retry loops: if the new wrapper budget is enabled, upstream callers must call with retries disabled (enforced in code path used by data-plane decrypt). 5) Add explicit metric/log fields so we can see retry amplification early (attempt_count, total_backoff_ms, deadline_remaining_ms, final_error_category).

Rationale: During the near-miss we saw KMS timeout rates rise, which triggered retries at multiple layers, increasing request volume to KMS and prolonging the incident. The most impactful short-term change is to keep retries but bound them to prevent amplification (availability) while still allowing a small number of attempts with jitter (latency + dependency health).

Detailed changes: - KMS retry policy for Timeout/Throttling is now: max_attempts=3 total, max_elapsed=900ms total per request (or earlier if ctx deadline is smaller), backoff base=50ms, max_backoff=250ms, full_jitter enabled. - AccessDenied/NotFound/InvalidCiphertext remain non-retryable. - Added `kms.retry_budget_exceeded` counter and `kms.retry_attempts` histogram; spans annotate `retry.attempt` and `retry.budget_remaining_ms`. - Added `x-redwood-kms-retry-budget` internal header propagation for the gateway -> service call (internal-only) so we can attribute budgets consistently in traces; this is only used on internal RPC and is not exposed to customers.

Files / areas touched: - services/kms/wrapper/retry_policy.go: new budgeted retry helper + full-jitter backoff - services/control-plane/crypto/envelope_decrypt.go: disables upstream retry loop when wrapper budget is enabled - services/api-gateway/middleware/timeouts.go: passes through request deadline to downstream client - pkg/telemetry/kms_metrics.go: new counters/histograms + trace attributes - configs/runtime_defaults.yaml: tighten defaults for timeout retry behavior in prod - tests/integration/kms_timeout_retry_budget_test.go: asserts no amplification (attempts <= 3, elapsed <= budget)

Illustrative snippet (retry budget logic): before: `for i in 0..5 { sleep(backoff(i)); callKMS() }` (no deadline check, no jitter, upstream also retried) after: `for attempt < max && now < deadline && elapsed < budget { sleep(jitter(expBackoff)); callKMS(ctxWithRemainingDeadline) }`

Rollout plan: - Merge + deploy as hotfix to canary in us-east-1 (5% traffic) - Watch dashboards: KMS dependency panel (latency p95, error rate, attempt_count distribution) and 5xx for decrypt path - If stable for 30m, roll to 50% then 100% (standard hotfix ramp) - Rollback trigger: decrypt error rate > 0.3% for 5m OR median attempt_count > 2.0 sustained OR overall request latency p95 increases > 10%

Follow-ups (not blocked on this hotfix): - ENG-4824: finalize circuit breaker + degrade-mode behavior so we fail fast when KMS is unhealthy (this hotfix only bounds retries). - ENG-4822: align final error taxonomy naming across logs/metrics (this adds fields but naming still needs the standard pass). - ENG-4828: tighten alert thresholds and add alert on retry-attempt distribution shift (amplification early warning). - Add explicit config guardrails so prod can’t be set to aggressive retry values without override (tracked separately).

Testing: - Unit: retry policy budget and jitter determinism via seeded RNG - Integration: simulated timeout KMS stub verifies max attempts and total elapsed cap - Manual: replayed staging traffic sample with fault injection (250ms timeouts) and verified attempt_count median <= 1, p95 <= 2

Release notes: Reduce KMS retry amplification on timeouts; add jittered backoff and retry-budget telemetry to prevent retry storms.

Co-authored-by: Nadia Rahman <nrahman@redwoodinference.com>

title: Incident intake: add required status page component/region/capability fields + validation
preview:
Why: We keep losing time (and occasionally accuracy) in the first 10–15 minutes of an incident because the intake form doesn’t capture the info required to pick the right public status page component(s). This led to coarse updates ("API") even when the impact was limited to Streaming or Embeddings in a single region. This PR updates the incident intake workflow to require three customer-facing classification fields (component, region, capability) and adds validation + reminders so Comms Lead can publish component-accurate updates quickly.

What changed (high level): - Adds new required intake fields: status_component_id, impacted_region, impacted_capability - Adds opinionated defaults + "Unknown/TBD" escape hatch to avoid blocking declaration - Adds server-side validation for required fields on incident creation + updates - Writes selected values into the incident record (used later by statuspage integration + summary generator) - Adds lightweight guardrails: if severity >= Sev2 and customer-facing, require the fields; if internal-only, allow empty but warn - Normalizes naming (component_id) to align with status component registry schema being added in observability-pack

User-facing behavior (Slack modal): - New section: "Customer-facing impact" - Component: single-select list (display names) mapping to status_component_id - Region: single-select (us-east, us-west, eu-west, ap-southeast, global) - Capability: single-select (chat, streaming, embeddings, reranking, console, dedicated-control-plane) - Includes helper text + link to component taxonomy doc

Validation rules: - For Sev0/Sev1/Sev2 incidents with "Customer-facing" = true: component + region + capability must be set (can be set to "unknown" values on first pass) - For Sev3+ or "Customer-facing" = false: fields optional, but modal shows a non-blocking warning when empty - On update: disallow clearing these fields for customer-facing incidents (prevents accidental regressions during handoffs)

Rollout notes: - Landed behind feature flag incident_intake.require_status_classification (default off) - Once enabled: the workflow still allows a fast declaration via the "Unknown/TBD" options; bot posts a reminder at +15m if unknown values remain for a customer-facing incident - This PR does not publish to Statuspage; it only captures structured fields (publishing comes in PR-19344)

Testing: - Unit tests for schema validation + backward compatibility for existing incidents without these fields - Manual: ran /incident declare in #incidents-sandbox and verified modal behavior across Sev1 vs Sev3 and customer-facing toggle

Screenshots: (internal) Slack modal before/after in the PR attachments

Related: - ENG-4821 (taxonomy) - ENG-4822 (statuspage integration) - Follow-up PR will backfill these fields on active incidents where possible

What changed (high level): - Adds new required intake fields: status_component_id, impacted_region, impacted_capability - Adds opinionated defaults + "Unknown/TBD" escape hatch to avoid blocking declaration - Adds server-side validation for required fields on incident creation + updates - Writes selected values into the incident record (used later by statuspage integration + summary generator) - Adds lightweight guardrails: if severity >= Sev2 and customer-facing, require the fields; if internal-only, allow empty but warn - Normalizes naming (component_id) to align with status component registry schema being added in observability-pack

User-facing behavior (Slack modal): - New section: "Customer-facing impact" - Component: single-select list (display names) mapping to status_component_id - Region: single-select (us-east, us-west, eu-west, ap-southeast, global) - Capability: single-select (chat, streaming, embeddings, reranking, console, dedicated-control-plane) - Includes helper text + link to component taxonomy doc

Validation rules: - For Sev0/Sev1/Sev2 incidents with "Customer-facing" = true: component + region + capability must be set (can be set to "unknown" values on first pass) - For Sev3+ or "Customer-facing" = false: fields optional, but modal shows a non-blocking warning when empty - On update: disallow clearing these fields for customer-facing incidents (prevents accidental regressions during handoffs)

Rollout notes: - Landed behind feature flag incident_intake.require_status_classification (default off) - Once enabled: the workflow still allows a fast declaration via the "Unknown/TBD" options; bot posts a reminder at +15m if unknown values remain for a customer-facing incident - This PR does not publish to Statuspage; it only captures structured fields (publishing comes in PR-19344)

Testing: - Unit tests for schema validation + backward compatibility for existing incidents without these fields - Manual: ran /incident declare in #incidents-sandbox and verified modal behavior across Sev1 vs Sev3 and customer-facing toggle

Screenshots: (internal) Slack modal before/after in the PR attachments

Related: - ENG-4821 (taxonomy) - ENG-4822 (statuspage integration) - Follow-up PR will backfill these fields on active incidents where possible

title: Add Comms Lead role assignment + TTFU reminders; log status updates to #incidents
preview: Context: We’ve had repeated incidents where we miss the first customer-facing update window (TTFU), and ownership of incident communications is ambiguous when the IC is deep in mitigation. This PR adds an explicit Comms Lead role to the incident-bot workflow, adds configurable reminders tied to severity (TTFU targets), and logs customer-facing update actions to #incidents for shared situational awareness and later audits. Changes: (1) Incident intake now captures/assigns a Comms Lead (default: secondary responder if present; else falls back to IC; can be reassigned). (2) Bot posts a lightweight “TTFU reminder” in the incident Slack channel if no status update has been logged within the target window (Sev0/Sev1 only by default). (3) Any status update action initiated via bot commands is echoed to #incidents with incident link + component(s) + state (Investigating/Identified/etc.) and the actor. (4) Reminders are suppressed for drills/test incidents and when the incident is marked as internal-only. Targets (configurable): Sev0=10m, Sev1=15m, Sev2=30m (Sev2 reminders disabled by default; can be enabled per team). Non-goals: This does not push to the public status page (that integration is tracked in ENG-4822); it only tracks/logs “customer-facing update” events and nudges the Comms Lead. Rollout: Behind feature flag INCIDENT_COMMS_LEAD_V1 (default off). Dogfood on SRE on-call rotation for 1 week; then expand to Infra on-call. Notes: Reminder messages are intentionally non-judgmental and include quick links to templates/runbook. Logging to #incidents avoids posting customer-specific details; the log line includes only incident ID, severity, impacted components (from intake), and phase.

title: Add overload error classification rules (separate from 5xx) for Hosted API SLO reporting
preview:
Context: As part of the Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding rollout, the gateway and downstream services now emit stable overload semantics (HTTP 429/503 + a Redwood error code + Retry-After). Our current SLO classifier treats most 5xx as availability failures, which makes overload-induced shedding look like an outage and obscures the intent of graceful degradation. This PR updates slo-toolkit classification rules so overload errors are tracked separately from generic 5xx while still being visible in dashboards/alerts.

What this changes: Adds a new classifier bucket `overload` and rules to map requests into it based on (1) the canonical Redwood error code (from `x-redwood-error-code` header or JSON body `error.code`) and (2) the overload HTTP status mapping (429/503 with overload code). Removes these responses from the default 5xx classifier so they no longer inflate `server_error` rates. Adds a policy switch to treat `overload` as excluded from the availability SLO calculation for Hosted API (per the updated SLO policy) while still emitting a parallel time series to quantify shed rate.

Signals supported: - Header: `x-redwood-error-code` in {`OVERLOAD_SHED`, `BREAKER_OPEN`, `CAPACITY_EXHAUSTED`, `DEPENDENCY_SATURATED`} - Body: `{"error":{"code":"overload_*"}}` (prefix match) - Status: 429/503 only count as overload when accompanied by an overload code (to avoid misclassifying generic 429 throttles)

Tests/validation: Adds fixtures using payloads from the gateway implementation PRs and validates (a) overload is not counted as 5xx, (b) availability burn excludes overload when `availability.exclude_overload=true`, (c) regression coverage for legacy 503s with no code still count as 5xx.

Rollout notes: This is a non-breaking change to the toolkit. Consumers should pull the latest rule bundle and confirm the new `overload` series is wired into dashboards. Follow-up work will update the default dashboard templates in observability-pack to include the new breakdown.

Refs: - Reliability program: Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding - ADR-014 overload error taxonomy - Related PRs: redwood#18421, redwood#18472 - Linear: ENG-4826 / ENG-4822

title: Hotfix: fix misclassified embeddings errors in status component map
preview:
Context
We discovered during a recent degradation + subsequent support escalations that embeddings request failures were being attributed to the Chat component in our public status taxonomy.

Impact
- Status automation and on-call updates were marking "Hosted API (Chat)" as degraded when the majority of errors were isolated to embeddings.
- "Hosted API (Embeddings)" could remain green even when /v1/embeddings was returning elevated 5xx (misleading for customers and Support).
- This also polluted the weekly status accuracy audit (component_id mismatch between incident tags and observed error distribution).

Root cause
The component classifier in the gateway error taxonomy used an ordered list of matchers. The chat matcher was too broad and was evaluated before the embeddings matcher. In particular:
- The chat matcher included a path prefix pattern that matched both /v1/chat/* and /v1/embeddings (via a legacy alias path normalization step).
- Because we short-circuit on first match, embeddings traffic was stamped with component_id=hosted_api.chat.

What changed
- Tightened the chat matcher to only match the chat + responses endpoints.
- Moved the embeddings matcher earlier in the evaluation chain and made it explicit for /v1/embeddings (+ legacy alias).
- Added regression tests covering the problematic paths and a few common variants.
- Added a small debug log (sampled) when component classification falls back to "hosted_api.core" to make future mismatches more discoverable without spamming logs.

Notes
This is intentionally scoped as a hotfix. We are not changing public component taxonomy here; just correcting the internal mapping feeding automation.

Verification
- Unit tests for path->component mapping.
- Manual replay: ran classifier against a small set of recorded request paths from the incident window (see comments).
- Confirmed status-page incident-bot draft now suggests the embeddings component for synthetic embeddings 5xx in staging.

Rollout
- Merged to main; will be included in the next gateway deploy (standard canary -> 100%).
- If any unexpected classification drift shows up in the audit job, we can revert quickly (changes isolated to component mapping + tests).

Checklist
- [x] Added regression tests
- [x] Updated on-call notes in code comment pointing to taxonomy doc
- [x] Verified staging classification for /v1/embeddings and /v1/chat/completions

Refs
- Support escalations: SUP-28411, SUP-28436
- Incident threads: #incidents (component map bug discovered mid-incident)
- Linear: ENG-4830 (service->component mapping), ENG-4821 (taxonomy roll-out)

Screenshots
N/A (code-only hotfix)
