title: Policy engine: overload rules, schema validation, and safe default tier policies (+ tenant config migration)
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds first-class overload protection rules to the policy engine, introduces strict schema validation (server-side) with safe defaults, and includes a one-time migration for existing tenant configs to the new `overload_policies` stanza.

## Motivation
As part of Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding (ENG-4821), the gateway and router need a single source of truth for tenant/tier/model-level overload behavior (admission caps, shed behavior, Retry-After hints, and optional degradation flags). We also need to prevent unsafe or foot-gun configurations (e.g., disabling shedding globally or setting unlimited concurrency for self-serve tiers) while still allowing per-tenant overrides for enterprise.

## What’s included
- New rule types under `overload_policies.v1`:
  - `admission_control`: token bucket + concurrency caps (per route/model selector)
  - `load_shedding`: shed response configuration (status mapping, Retry-After bounds, error code override policy)
  - `degradation_flags`: allow/deny list for feature reduction under duress (e.g., enforce `max_tokens` cap, streaming concurrency limit)
  - `breaker_thresholds`: threshold hooks (e.g., queue depth, scheduler backlog) as named signal references (actual signal evaluation stays in gateway/runtime; policy engine owns config)
- Schema validation + guardrails:
  - Enforce upper bounds for concurrency/max_tokens by tier (self-serve defaults are stricter)
  - Require explicit allowlist for any degradation mode (default deny)
  - Disallow selectors that would match “all tenants” unless caller is privileged (protects tenant isolation)
  - Validate Retry-After range (min/max) and forbid missing retry guidance when `shed_behavior=retryable`
  - Validate model selectors resolve to known catalog IDs (or `*` only where allowed)
- Safe default policies:
  - Introduce tier defaults (`self_serve`, `hosted_enterprise`, `dedicated_hosted`) applied when tenant config omits `overload_policies`
  - Defaults are intentionally conservative and can be overridden per tenant via existing config override flow
- Migration:
  - Adds a migration that backfills `overload_policies.v1` into existing tenant config documents with the tier defaults
  - Includes a “dry-run” validator mode used during migration to log-but-not-block when legacy fields are present; after migration, validation is strict for new writes
- Tests:
  - Golden tests for schema validation error messages
  - Migration unit tests (idempotency, rollback behavior)
  - RBAC tests for privileged “global selector” usage

## Rollout / operational notes
- Feature is behind `policy_engine.overload_policies_v1_enabled` (default ON in staging, OFF in prod until gateway wiring lands).
- Migration runs in read-repair mode during the first config fetch/write cycle; no mass rewrite job.
- Added metrics:
  - `policy_engine.validation_errors_total{domain="overload"}`
  - `policy_engine.migration_applied_total{name="overload_policies_v1"}`

## Follow-ups
- Gateway middleware will start consuming these rules for admission control (ENG-4823).
- Smart routing will consume fallback config (separate PR; this PR only establishes policy scaffolding).

## Screenshots / examples
Example tenant override (abridged):
- Self-serve: `concurrency_cap=12`, `retry_after_seconds=[1,8]`, `degradation_flags=[]`
- Hosted enterprise: `concurrency_cap=80`, `retry_after_seconds=[1,3]`, `degradation_flags=["cap_max_tokens","limit_stream_concurrency"]`

## Checklist
- [x] Backwards compatible (new fields additive)
- [x] Validation messages are actionable (include JSON pointer + tier)
- [x] Migration is idempotent + safe on partial failures
- [x] Unit tests added
- [x] Docs comments included for config authors

title: Add KMS dependency healthcheck endpoint + SRE hooks (predeploy gate)
preview:
Summary
- Adds an internal KMS dependency healthcheck endpoint for services that rely on envelope decryption.
- Exposes standardized SRE hooks so rollout tooling can gate deploys on KMS readiness and SRE can alert on sustained degradation.

Motivation / context
We’ve had a few cases (and a recent us-east timeout spike) where KMS latency/throttling started degrading request paths, but we didn’t have a single, low-noise signal that answers: “is KMS healthy enough for this service to safely take traffic right now?”

This PR implements an internal healthcheck that:
- does NOT call KMS on each request (avoids amplification / self-inflicted throttling)
- distinguishes transient vs sustained failures
- standardizes output so alerting + predeploy checks can consume it consistently

Design highlights
- Endpoint: GET /internal/health/kms
  - Returns 200 when KMS is healthy per local probe state
  - Returns 503 when probe has been failing beyond a configurable grace window
  - Includes machine-readable fields for automation (status, last_success_age_s, last_error_kind, last_error_message, probe_mode)
- Probe model: asynchronous background probe with caching
  - Runs every N seconds (default 30s)
  - Timeout per probe call (default 750ms) to avoid hanging the probe loop
  - Tracks last success timestamp + last failure reason (mapped via the canonical KMS error taxonomy)
  - Exposes a gauge for “kms_dependency_healthy” and counters for “kms_probe_errors_total{kind=...}”
- Probe modes
  1) decrypt_only (default for data-plane services)
     - Requires kms:Decrypt only
     - Decrypts a small, non-production probe ciphertext provided via config/secret (generated during provisioning)
     - Avoids requiring kms:Encrypt or DescribeKey permissions at runtime
  2) describe_key (opt-in)
     - Calls DescribeKey / GetKeyRotationStatus where permissions exist (control-plane / some private deployments)

What changed
- New internal route handler wired under existing internal mux: /internal/health/kms
- New KmsDependencyProber background task
- New config block:
  - kms.healthcheck.enabled (default true for control-plane; false for batch jobs)
  - kms.healthcheck.mode (decrypt_only|describe_key)
  - kms.healthcheck.interval (default 30s)
  - kms.healthcheck.timeout (default 750ms)
  - kms.healthcheck.fail_after (default 120s) (how long we tolerate sustained probe failures before reporting unhealthy)
  - kms.healthcheck.probe_ciphertext_ref (required for decrypt_only)
- SRE hooks:
  - Adds dependency status to the internal “/internal/sre/deps” payload (consumed by deploy tooling)
  - Adds a lightweight predeploy gate helper in the deploy-checks library that fails closed when KMS is unhealthy for > fail_after
- Observability:
  - Metrics:
    - redwood_kms_probe_latency_ms{mode,env,region}
    - redwood_kms_probe_errors_total{kind,mode,env,region}
    - redwood_kms_dependency_healthy{mode,env,region} (0/1)
  - Logs: structured log on state transition (healthy->unhealthy and unhealthy->healthy) to avoid log spam

Example response
200 OK
{"status":"ok","dependency":"kms","probe_mode":"decrypt_only","last_success_age_s":12,"last_error_kind":"","last_error_message":""}

503 Service Unavailable
{"status":"degraded","dependency":"kms","probe_mode":"decrypt_only","last_success_age_s":187,"last_error_kind":"Timeout","last_error_message":"context deadline exceeded"}

How to test
- Unit tests cover:
  - state transitions and fail_after grace behavior
  - error kind mapping -> endpoint output
  - probe loop jitter and timeout enforcement
- Manual:
  1) Start service with kms.healthcheck.enabled=true and a valid probe ciphertext ref
  2) Hit /internal/health/kms (should be 200 once the first probe succeeds)
  3) Break permissions / point to invalid key and confirm:
     - endpoint stays 200 for < fail_after
     - then flips to 503
     - logs one transition line

Rollout / safety
- Endpoint is internal-only (behind existing internal auth/mTLS) and not exposed publicly.
- Probe loop is rate-limited by interval; no per-request KMS call path.
- If probe is misconfigured (e.g., missing probe ciphertext), healthcheck reports “degraded” with a config error kind, but does not crash the service.

Follow-ups
- ENG-4828: wire these metrics into the KMS dashboards + paging thresholds (this PR provides the primitives).
- Private installer: provision/generate probe ciphertext artifacts automatically during install (tracked separately).

title: Add burst traffic scenario to validate overload shedding vs timeouts
preview:
Summary: This PR adds a new perf-canary scenario that generates bursty, highly-variable request load (concurrency ramp + prompt-length ramp) against Hosted API routes to validate overload protection behavior. The goal is to ensure we see explicit overload responses (429/503 w/ stable error codes + Retry-After) and graceful degradation instead of tail-latency blowups/timeouts during bursts.

Context: As part of the Hosted circuit breakers + load shedding reliability program (ENG-4821), we need a repeatable canary that exercises the failure mode we see in real incidents: fast concurrency spikes plus long prompts causing prefill pressure and queue backlogs. We previously only had steady-state throughput scenarios, which are not good at catching retry storms/timeouts. This scenario is intended to be used in dark launch + regional canary gates.

What’s included: 1) New scenario: burst_overload_ramp. Phases: (a) Warm-up steady load, (b) Concurrency ramp 50 -> 600 over ~6 minutes with jittered arrival, (c) Prompt length ramp (p50 ~600 toks, p95 ~6k toks) while holding concurrency high, (d) Recovery ramp-down to verify breaker close and latency returns. Routes: /v1/chat/completions (streaming ON + OFF variants) and /v1/completions (non-stream) behind a single scenario config switch. Models: configurable, defaulting to the primary hosted chat model and a fallback-eligible model pair when enabled. Regions: parameterized (default us-east) for canary rollouts.

2) Assertions/health checks: - Latency SLO guard: p95 and p99 tracked per-phase; scenario fails if p99 latency exceeds threshold for >N consecutive windows while shed rate remains low (signals we are timing out rather than shedding). - Timeout/transport errors: hard fail if client-side timeouts or connection resets exceed a small budget, since overload should be expressed as an explicit error response. - Overload response contract: when we get 429/503, validate JSON payload contains (a) stable overload error code, (b) request/correlation ID, and (c) recommended retry guidance. Validate Retry-After header is present for overload responses (warn -> fail in phase C if missing). - Retry-storm avoidance: scenario client respects Retry-After and applies exponential backoff + jitter for idempotent retries only; by default, we do not retry streaming requests.

3) Metrics export: - Adds a scenario-local summary of: overload_rate, breaker_open_rate (derived from response codes/payload), timeout_rate, and per-phase latency deltas. - Writes a small JSON summary artifact consumed by the canary runner so SRE can gate rollouts on the key numbers without spelunking logs.

4) Docs: - Adds a short README snippet for running locally and recommended flags for canary environments (region/model overrides, max_rps guardrails, and how to disable retries for incident simulations).

Implementation details: - Workload generation uses our existing arrival scheduler but adds: (a) phase-aware target concurrency, (b) per-request prompt size distribution (log-normal-ish) to mimic real prompt variance, and (c) optional streaming mode toggle. - Response validation looks for overload error codes in the JSON body (string match for now) and validates Retry-After parsing. This is intentionally strict to catch regressions like “429 without header” seen in SUP-2860 and “503 without code” which leads to ambiguous client behavior. - Kept dimensions low to avoid high-cardinality metrics. Scenario emits only per-phase aggregates plus a small set of tagged counters (region, route, streaming=true/false).

Files changed (high level): - scenarios/burst_overload_ramp.yaml (new) - scenarios/burst_overload_ramp.streaming.yaml (new) - runner/scenario_loader.go (add schema fields: phases, prompt_profile, streaming toggle) - workload/prompt_profile.go (new: prompt length distribution helpers) - validation/overload_contract.go (new: Retry-After + error payload checks) - cmd/perf-canary/README.md (updated) - tests/overload_contract_test.go (new)

How to run: - Local quick run (safe): perf-canary run --scenario burst_overload_ramp --duration 12m --region us-east --max-rps 120 --dry-run=false - Streaming variant: perf-canary run --scenario burst_overload_ramp.streaming --duration 12m --region us-east - Suggested canary gate: run in canary env with a capped max-rps and compare (timeout_rate + p99) against baseline; overload_rate is expected to increase during peak phases.

Risk/rollout: This only affects perf-canary. The default scenario set is unchanged; the new scenario must be explicitly invoked. We keep conservative defaults (max_rps cap, no retries for streaming) to prevent accidental pressure on prod.

Checklist: - [x] Scenario config reviewed with SRE (Rafael) - [x] Retry/backoff behavior respects Retry-After - [x] Fails on timeouts/connection errors beyond budget - [x] CI green - [x] Added unit tests for Retry-After parsing and payload requirements

Review notes: Rafael Mendes: “Can we make the failure mode explicit? I want the scenario to fail if timeouts rise but shed stays near zero (meaning breaker isn’t firing). Also please ensure we don’t retry streaming; it masks the issue and is not what customers do.” Christine Wong: “Done. Added a guardrail: if p99 spikes + timeout_rate > 0.5% in phase B/C while overload_rate < 0.2%, we fail with a message pointing to the likely regression (breaker not opening / admission not shedding). Also disabled retries for streaming by default; non-stream retries are capped and honor Retry-After.” Aisha Bello: “Please keep summary dimensions minimal; we’ve had cardinality blowups when scenarios emit per-prompt-length tags.” Christine Wong: “Ack. Only emits per-phase aggregates and route/streaming booleans; prompt lengths are only reported as histogram buckets locally in the JSON artifact (not as tagged metrics).” Sofia Ivanova: “Prompt length ramp: can you align to the runtime team’s expectation (long prefill is the pain point). Also, would be great if we track a prefill-like marker even client-side.” Christine Wong: “Adjusted prompt distribution so p95 is long enough to hit prefill pressure and added a client-side ‘time_to_first_token’ measurement for streaming; included in the summary artifact for phase C.”

CI: - build: pass - unit-tests: pass - lint: pass - integration smoke (staging endpoint): pass

Merge: Squash merged to main.

Follow-ups: - Add optional dependency brownout phase (simulate downstream latency) as part of game day scripts (ENG-4830). - Once ADR-014 error payload is fully rolled out, tighten payload validation from string match -> structured schema validation.

title: Add standardized overload error codes + JSON payload (Hosted API)
preview:
Context: During overload we currently surface a mix of upstream timeouts, generic 503s, and ad-hoc JSON bodies depending on which hop rejects the request (gateway vs routing vs runtime admission). That makes client behavior inconsistent and increases retry storms (clients retry immediately on timeouts/unknown 5xx). This PR introduces a single canonical overload error response struct and a stable error-code taxonomy for Hosted API overload/load-shedding. Goal is to make overload failures explicit, machine-parseable, and consistent across the request path while remaining backwards compatible (additive fields only). This aligns with ADR-014 (Hosted overload error taxonomy) and sets up follow-on work in the gateway middleware + routing fallback (ENG-4823/ENG-4824).

What changed: 1) Add `OverloadErrorResponse` (shared API error type) with stable fields: `error.type=overload_error`, `error.code` (stable), `error.message` (customer-safe), `error.retryable` (bool), `error.retry_after_ms` (optional), `error.request_id` (correlation id), `error.details` (optional, bounded). 2) Add mapping from internal reasons (gateway/routing/runtime) to stable `OverloadErrorCode` values. We explicitly avoid leaking internal component names and ensure codes don’t change as thresholds/tuning changes. 3) Standardize HTTP status mapping: 429 for tenant/tier admission limits (quota/concurrency/rate-limits), 503 for platform capacity / downstream saturation / brownouts. Status mapping is determined by the stable code, not by the caller. 4) Add unit tests + golden JSON snapshots for response payloads and headers. 5) Add doc-comments / internal “client retry contract” block to guide SDK authors: respect `Retry-After` header when present, use exponential backoff + jitter, do not retry non-idempotent requests without idempotency keys.

Stable error codes added (initial set): - `overload_tenant_concurrency_limit` (429) - `overload_tenant_rate_limit` (429) - `overload_tier_capacity` (503) - `overload_region_capacity` (503) - `overload_runtime_queue_saturated` (503) - `overload_runtime_memory_pressure` (503) - `overload_dependency_unhealthy` (503)

Example response (503): `{ "error": { "type": "overload_error", "code": "overload_region_capacity", "message": "Service is temporarily overloaded. Please retry after a short delay.", "retryable": true, "retry_after_ms": 1200, "request_id": "req_01H..." } }` and `Retry-After: 1` (seconds). Example response (429) uses the same body shape with a 429 status.

Compatibility: This is additive to existing error JSON (we keep `error.message` semantics and don’t remove existing top-level keys). Callers that don’t understand the new fields will continue to parse `message`. Follow-up PRs will adopt this struct in the gateway admission middleware and routing fallback paths so we stop emitting legacy overload bodies.

Notes: - `error.details` is intentionally restricted to low-cardinality values (e.g., `policy_id`, `limit_type`) to avoid leaking sensitive info and avoid observability cardinality blowups. - `request_id` is sourced from existing request context; if missing we generate one at the edge (existing behavior).

Release notes (internal): Hosted API overload errors now include stable `error.code` values and consistent JSON payload shape; clients should treat these as retryable only when `retryable=true` and should honor `Retry-After` when present.

Refs: ADR-014 Hosted overload error taxonomy; Linear ENG-4822.

title: Add incident timeline export hook for post-incident summary generation
preview:
Context: As part of the Status Page Transparency Upgrades work, we need a reliable, structured export of incident timeline events (state changes, mitigations, key decisions) so we can auto-draft publishable post-incident summaries and run accuracy audits. Today the incident service stores the timeline in the DB and renders it in the internal UI, but there is no stable export contract and downstream consumers have been scraping. This PR introduces a first-class export hook in the `incident-api` service that emits a canonical stream of timeline events and exposes a read API for the summary generator.

What changed: (1) Added a new `IncidentTimelineEventV1` schema with a stable set of fields (incident_id, event_id, occurred_at, actor, event_type, state_from/state_to, summary, details_redacted, tags). (2) Added an append-only export hook that publishes events to `incident.timeline.v1` via our internal event bus whenever timeline entries are created/updated, including state transitions and mitigation notes. (3) Added a paginated read endpoint `GET /internal/incidents/:id/timeline/export` to support backfills and deterministic replays. (4) Added redaction guardrails: fields flagged as `sensitive` are excluded from the export payload by default and require an explicit `include_sensitive=true` flag that is blocked in production by config. (5) Added basic idempotency + ordering: event_id is stable (UUIDv7) and we include a monotonic `sequence` derived from the incident timeline row version to reduce out-of-order issues for consumers.

Why this design: We want the summary generator to be able to reconstruct an incident narrative without being tightly coupled to our DB schema or UI formatting. Publishing on write keeps TTFU-related tooling (incident-bot reminders, comms drafting) aligned with the same timeline that the post-incident summary uses. The read endpoint exists primarily for backfill/replay and for the weekly status accuracy audit job.

Non-goals: This does not implement the post-incident summary generator itself (ENG-4823), nor does it add any status page automation. It also does not export raw metrics; downstream consumers should use dashboards/SLO data directly.

Rollout plan: Behind feature flag `INCIDENT_TIMELINE_EXPORT_ENABLED` (default off). Stage in dev + staging this week; enable in prod for SRE-only incidents first. Observability team will add consumer lag/throughput panels once enabled.

Risk notes: Biggest risk is leaking sensitive details into exported payloads. This PR errs on the side of redaction by default and includes tests that ensure `details_redacted=true` for mitigation notes containing links to customer tickets, hostnames, or capacity identifiers.

Follow-ups: ENG-4823 will consume this stream to draft publishable post-incident summaries. ENG-4830 will align incident-bot tagging so exported events include component_id/region/capability consistently. PM-612 will document what fields are used in the publishable summary and what is never exported.

Test plan: Unit tests for schema + redaction; integration tests verifying publish-on-write and replay endpoint; manual staging test by creating an incident, flipping states, adding mitigation note, confirming events in `eventbus-debug` and verifying replay matches published payload.

Release notes: Internal only — adds an internal export contract for incident timeline events to support automated post-incident summaries.

title: Add KMS latency/error dashboards + alert rules (per env/region)
preview: Context: As part of the KMS integration hardening initiative we needed first-class dependency visibility for KMS across Hosted/Dedicated/Private paths. This PR adds a KMS Dependency Health dashboard and alert rules that align to the canonical error taxonomy (AccessDenied/NotFound/Throttling/Timeout/InvalidCiphertext/Unknown) and our metric namespacing conventions. Scope: (1) Dashboard: Overview + per env/region panels for request volume, latency (p50/p95/p99), error rate by category, throttling signals, and circuit breaker state; (2) Alerts: page-worthy vs ticket-worthy thresholds, with environment-aware routing (prod pages, staging tickets) and burn-rate style windows where appropriate; (3) Operator ergonomics: runbook links + log/trace pivot hints (trace_id, kms_key_id, provider, region, service); (4) Documentation: short README explaining how to use/tune the alerts and what to check first during an incident. Recommended thresholds (initial): Latency p95: warn >250ms for 10m, page >500ms for 5m (prod only); Timeout error rate: warn >0.25% for 10m, page >0.75% for 5m; Throttling: warn >0.5% for 10m, page >1.5% for 10m (with annotation to check retry storm/backpressure); AccessDenied: page on sustained >10/min for 5m (prod) because it often indicates mis-scoped IAM or a rollout regression; InvalidCiphertext: page on any sustained nonzero (>1/min for 5m) because it can indicate key mismatch/rotation cutover issues. Notes: thresholds are intentionally conservative and will be tuned post-rollout (see ENG-4828 follow-up and the alert noise discussion). This PR assumes the KMS metrics emitted by the runtime/control plane are available (redwood PR-18434 / PR-18515 / PR-18527) and uses the standardized tags: env, region, service, provider, error_category, error_code, kms_key_id (hashed).

title: Implement KMS circuit breaker + fallback mode to prevent retry storms during KMS incidents
preview:
Context: During recent KMS throttling/timeouts (see SUP-19377), application-side retries amplified load and created a request storm against KMS, worsening tail latency and elevating 5xx. We’ve already standardized timeouts/retries in the KMS wrapper (PR #18421); this change adds circuit breaking + a documented degrade/fallback policy so we fail fast and shed load instead of stampeding KMS.

What this PR does:
- Adds a per-key (key ARN) circuit breaker in the shared KMS client wrapper with CLOSED/OPEN/HALF_OPEN states.
- Introduces a fallback mode policy for decrypt/envelope operations:
  - If circuit is OPEN: do not call KMS; attempt to serve from in-memory DEK cache if present (bounded TTL, non-persistent); otherwise fail fast with a mapped KMSUnavailable error (no retries).
  - Encrypt/new key generation paths: fail fast when OPEN (no cache fallback), returning a distinct error class to avoid queuing / retry loops.
- Caps concurrency to KMS with a small in-flight semaphore (prevents spikes during HALF_OPEN).
- Emits metrics + logs needed for on-call triage:
  - redwood.kms.circuit_breaker.state (gauge)
  - redwood.kms.circuit_breaker.open_total / half_open_total (counters)
  - redwood.kms.circuit_breaker.blocked_total (counter)
  - Structured log fields: kms.key_arn, kms.op, kms.error_category, kms.circuit_state, kms.circuit_open_reason
- Adds unit tests with fake clock to validate: state transitions, half-open probe behavior, and that OPEN short-circuits before any retry logic.
- Documents the operational behavior in-code (module docs + inline comment block) with explicit non-goals: we are not changing cryptographic primitives, not persisting plaintext key material, and not altering key rotation semantics.

SRE-requested changes included here:
- HALF_OPEN uses jittered probe scheduling to avoid synchronized thundering herd after an incident clears.
- OPEN duration is bounded; we re-check via controlled probes rather than staying OPEN indefinitely.
- Separate breaker keys for decrypt vs metadata calls (avoid a metadata blip taking down decrypt).
- Explicit error mapping for OPEN-short-circuit -> KMS_CIRCUIT_OPEN (distinct from timeout), and we do not apply caller retry policy to this class.

Config (defaults are safe for prod):
- kms.circuit_breaker.enabled=true
- kms.circuit_breaker.failure_threshold=8 (rolling window)
- kms.circuit_breaker.window=30s
- kms.circuit_breaker.open_cooldown=45s (+/- 20% jitter)
- kms.circuit_breaker.half_open_max_in_flight=2
- kms.circuit_breaker.cache_fallback_ttl=5m (decrypt only; bounded, memory-only)

Rollout notes:
- This is behind a feature flag kms.circuit_breaker.enabled (on by default in staging; gradual enable in prod per-region).
- Please coordinate with ENG-4828 (dashboard/alerts) before broad prod enablement.

Test plan:
- Unit tests for breaker transitions and short-circuit behavior.
- Staging: simulate KMS 429/timeout using the fault injection harness; verify: request rate to KMS drops sharply once OPEN, p95 latency stabilizes, and error responses are consistent and actionable.

Follow-ups (not in this PR):
- Update runbook playbook examples to include KMS_CIRCUIT_OPEN and recommended mitigations (ENG-4821 docs workstream).
- Extend integration tests to assert breaker behavior under sustained throttling (ENG-4825).

Screenshots / evidence:
- Added a short “expected graphs” note to help validate in staging: look for breaker_open_total increments and a drop in kms.request_total during simulated failures.

title: Add integration test harness for KMS failure modes (timeouts/throttling) + retry/latency assertions
preview: Context: As part of the KMS hardening initiative (ENG-4821/ENG-4825) we need repeatable, non-flaky coverage for the most common real-world failure modes we’ve seen (timeouts and throttling) and guardrails to prevent retry amplification. This PR introduces an integration-test harness that can deterministically inject KMS failures and validate that our KMS wrapper obeys configured budgets (max retries + max wall-clock) and that upstream services surface the correct error category without masking (e.g., Throttling vs Timeout vs AccessDenied). What’s included: (1) A small “kms-fault-injector” sidecar used only in integration tests (runs in docker-compose and the CI integration job). It proxies KMS requests and can inject (a) fixed latency, (b) probabilistic timeouts (connection stall), and (c) throttling responses (AWS-style ThrottlingException / HTTP 429 equivalent) based on per-test configuration. (2) New integration tests for envelope decrypt paths in the control-plane and data-plane adapters that cover: timeout -> retry with jitter -> fail fast within latency cap; throttling -> bounded retries -> correct error taxonomy; and “mixed” failure where first N requests throttle then succeed, asserting the wrapper eventually succeeds without exceeding retry budget. (3) Assertions for total wall-clock budget and retry counts using emitted metrics/spans from the wrapper: kms.client.attempts (counter), kms.client.latency_ms (histogram), kms.client.errors{category=...} (counter). These assertions are done by reading the local in-memory test metrics exporter (not scraping Prometheus) to keep tests hermetic. (4) A CI job wiring (integration-kms-failure-modes) that runs these tests in parallel with other integration suites, gated on main and release/*, and skips on fork PRs (requires docker service). How to run locally: `make itest-kms-failure-modes` (requires docker). Notes/constraints: This is AWS-mode only for now; the harness is intentionally provider-agnostic at the proxy layer, but the error-shape fixtures currently match AWS responses. We’ll extend with GCP/Azure once those wrappers land behind the same error taxonomy (ENG-4822). We intentionally avoid testing circuit-breaker behavior here; that is covered in ENG-4824/PR-18449. Risk: Low product risk (test-only code paths), moderate flake risk; mitigations include deterministic seeds, fixed injection windows, and conservative latency thresholds. Evidence: This PR adds regression coverage for SUP-19377 patterns (intermittent 5xx correlated with KMS throttling) and enforces the retry cap/latency cap behavior agreed in ADR-007.

title: Add incident template/playbook for Provision-to-Ready (GPU provisioning) failures
preview:
## Summary
Adds a dedicated incident template + lightweight playbook for Provision-to-Ready / Dedicated fleet bring-up failures (nodes stuck in Provisioning/Validating, mass quarantine, image/driver mismatch, join failures, NCCL smoke flakes).

This is intended to standardize the minimum info we capture in #incidents and in the incident doc so SRE/Infra/Platform can triage quickly and we can compute time-to-ready and failure-rate impacts without back-and-forth.

## Why
During the staged rollout we saw a few common patterns:
- Missing pool metadata (region/GPU type/image channel), which slowed down correlating failures to a specific rollout.
- Unclear ownership handoff (Infra vs Platform vs SRE) depending on whether the failure is “can’t join cluster” vs “validation gate failing” vs “readiness service/API regressions”.
- Links to dashboards/runbooks living in different places and being pasted inconsistently.

This PR adds:
1) A new incident template “Provision-to-Ready / Fleet Readiness failure” in incident-bot.
2) Required fields (bot enforces) for the most important scoping + debugging info.
3) One-page playbook section (first 10 minutes) with quick links and suggested owner rotations.

## Template: required fields
- Incident start time (UTC) + detection source (alert name / human report)
- Customer impact statement (Dedicated pools impacted? capacity shortfall? time-to-ready regression?)
- Affected region(s)
- Pool ID(s) / cluster(s)
- GPU type(s) (A100/H100/etc)
- Image channel + version (candidate/stable + build id)
- Current readiness states distribution (Allocated/Provisioning/Validating/Ready/Quarantined)
- Failing step(s) + top failure reason(s) (e.g., kubelet join, driver parity, NCCL smoke, perf canary)
- Rollout flags enabled? (region/pool constraints)
- Immediate mitigation chosen (pause rollout, reimage, drain/quarantine override, rollback image channel)

## Playbook: first 10 minutes
- Confirm scope using the readiness summary endpoint (counts by state + top failures)
- Check Provision-to-Ready dashboards (step latency + failure rate heatmap)
- Determine whether failure is:
  A) image bake / driver parity (Infra/Release Eng)
  B) cluster join / node config baseline (Infra)
  C) readiness service / state machine / API issues (Platform)
  D) alerting/metrics/cardinality issues (SRE/Observability)
- Apply “safe” mitigations first:
  - Pause staged rollout flag(s)
  - Stop auto-promotion (candidate->stable)
  - Quarantine only if repeatable hardware/validation failure; avoid mass quarantine until capacity impact is understood
  - Reimage a small sample and re-run validation to confirm whether image rollback is warranted

## Suggested owner rotations
- Incident Commander (IC): SRE oncall
- Primary Responder: Infra/Fleet oncall
- Secondary: Platform Control Plane oncall (readiness service)
- Consult: Release Engineering (image channel/promotion), Security (if image hardening or access control suspected)

## Notes
- Links are intentionally internal and stable (dashboards + runbook + readiness service doc). If any of these URLs change we should update the template rather than relying on manual pastes.

## Screenshots
N/A (template-only change).

## Rollout
Once merged, incident-bot will pick up the new template on next deploy (no config changes required in prod).

title: Quarantine nodes on repeat validation failures (safe defaults + operator override)
preview:
## Summary
This PR implements automatic quarantine for nodes that repeatedly fail provision-to-ready validation. The goal is to prevent noisy/flaky nodes from looping forever in Validating and then accidentally joining serving pools, while keeping defaults conservative and providing explicit operator override pathways.

## Motivation / Context
During staged rollout we saw a subset of nodes repeatedly failing the same validation step (most commonly: driver parity drift, GPU health/Xid, and NCCL smoke failures). Retrying indefinitely increases time-to-ready, consumes orchestrator capacity, and creates ambiguous operator outcomes ("is this a transient flake or a bad node?").

This change adds:
- A canonical definition of "repeat validation failure" (consecutive failures of the same validation category within a time window)
- Automatic transition to `Quarantined` after the threshold is exceeded
- An audited operator override endpoint to unquarantine with a reason + TTL, plus guardrails to prevent silent re-entry

## Behavior changes
### When we quarantine
A node is quarantined when ALL of the following are true:
- The node is currently in `Validating` (or `Provisioning` with post-flight validations enabled)
- A "hard" validation category fails consecutively >= threshold
- Failures occur within the configured window (default 60 minutes) and are not separated by a successful run of that category

"Hard" categories (default):
- `gpu_health` (ECC/Xid thresholds)
- `driver_parity` (expected NVIDIA driver/CUDA toolkit mismatch)
- `nccl_smoke` (fabric/all-reduce sanity)

"Soft" categories (default, never auto-quarantine):
- `artifact_upload` / log bundle upload
- transient `network_probe` timeouts
- control-plane write retries

### Safe defaults
- `QUARANTINE_CONSECUTIVE_FAILURES=3`
- `QUARANTINE_WINDOW=60m`
- Quarantine is scoped per node and per validation category, but the quarantine decision is node-level (i.e., we quarantine the node after the threshold in any hard category).
- Quarantine is only applied when the orchestrator has a stable node identity (node_id assigned) to avoid quarantining "unknown" resources.

### Operator override
Adds internal admin endpoints:
- `POST /internal/fleet/nodes/{node_id}:unquarantine`
  - Requires elevated role (fleet-ops) and a non-empty `reason`
  - Supports optional `ttl_minutes` (default 120, max 1440)
  - Writes an audit log entry and emits a metric

Unquarantine does NOT auto-mark Ready; it simply moves the node back to `Provisioning` (or `Validating` depending on where it failed) and forces a full re-run of hard validations.

## Implementation notes
- Adds a small "failure accumulator" to the readiness state store. We persist:
  - last_failed_category
  - consecutive_failure_count
  - last_failure_at
  - quarantine_reason (if quarantined)
  - quarantined_at
  - unquarantine_until (if overridden)
- Updates the state machine transition guard:
  - `Validating -> Quarantined` when threshold exceeded
  - `Quarantined -> Provisioning` only via explicit operator action (or TTL expiry with feature flag off; see below)
- Adds metrics (low-cardinality):
  - `fleet_readiness_quarantine_total{category,reason_code}`
  - `fleet_readiness_unquarantine_total{actor}`
  - `fleet_readiness_validation_consecutive_failures{category}` (gauge)

## Feature flag / rollout
- Default enabled for Dedicated pools only via existing pool-level staged rollout flag.
- Kill-switch: `fleet.readiness.quarantine_enabled` (global) and `fleet.readiness.quarantine_enabled_by_pool` (pool override)

## Testing
- Unit tests for accumulator semantics (windowing + reset on success)
- State-machine transition tests (no quarantine on soft categories)
- Authz tests for unquarantine endpoint
- Manual test plan:
  - Force driver mismatch -> confirm quarantine after 3 runs
  - Unquarantine with TTL -> confirm validations re-run and audit event recorded

## Follow-ups
- Add runbook section with concrete remediation steps per category (reimage vs RMA vs network ticket)
- Consider auto-creating a Linear incident/task when quarantines spike per pool

title: Add time-to-ready SLI calculation (provisioning -> Ready) from event timestamps
preview:
Title: Add time-to-ready SLI calculation (provisioning -> Ready) from event timestamps

Context
We’re rolling out provision-to-ready automation for Dedicated pools, and we need a canonical SLI for “time-to-ready” that is consistent across regions/GPU types and robust to retries/quarantine. This PR adds an SLI calculator in slo-toolkit that derives duration from the event stream produced by the provisioning orchestrator and fleet readiness service.

What this PR does
- Introduces a new SLI: time_to_ready_seconds (aka provision_to_ready_seconds) computed per node instance based on event timestamps.
- Uses event timestamps rather than scrape-time to avoid skew and to survive delayed ingestion.
- Defines the start and end markers as:
  - start: first Observed(Allocated) or ProvisioningStarted event for a node (prefers Allocated when present)
  - end: first Ready event (terminal Ready transition) for that node’s “bring-up attempt”
- Adds normalization logic to handle out-of-order events, duplicates, and multiple attempts.
- Adds guardrails so quarantined nodes do not silently poison the SLI distribution (we record as outcome=quarantined and exclude from success SLI, but still emit a separate failure duration).
- Adds unit tests and golden fixtures covering edge cases observed during baseline measurement.

SLI definition details (aligned with ENG-4821)
- SLI name: provision_to_ready_time
- Measure: (ready_ts - allocated_ts) in seconds
- Population: nodes in Dedicated and shared fleets that emit readiness events (pool_type tag)
- Success criteria: node reaches Ready within SLO window (SLO enforcement lives elsewhere; this PR only computes the raw duration distribution + outcomes)
- Outcome tags:
  - outcome=ready (terminal success)
  - outcome=quarantined (terminal failure)
  - outcome=unknown (insufficient data; excluded from SLI)

Implementation notes
- Added EventWindowResolver to group events into a single bring-up attempt. Rules:
  - Prefer the earliest allocated/provisioning_start timestamp as start.
  - If a node emits multiple allocated/provisioning_start events, treat them as retries within the same attempt unless a ReimageRequested or NodeReplaced event is present (then start a new attempt window).
  - If Ready occurs before start due to out-of-order ingestion, clamp by sorting on event_ts; if still inconsistent, mark unknown and emit a debug counter.

Context
We’re rolling out provision-to-ready automation for Dedicated pools, and we need a canonical SLI for “time-to-ready” that is consistent across regions/GPU types and robust to retries/quarantine. What this PR does

- Uses event timestamps rather than scrape-time to avoid skew and to survive delayed ingestion.
- Defines the start and end markers as:
  - start: first Observed(Allocated) or ProvisioningStarted event for a node (prefers Allocated when present)
  - end: first Ready event (terminal Ready transition) for that node’s “bring-up attempt”
- Adds normalization logic to handle out-of-order events, duplicates, and multiple attempts.
- Adds guardrails so quarantined nodes do not silently poison the SLI distribution (we record as outcome=quarantined and exclude from success SLI, but still emit a separate failure duration).
- Adds unit tests and golden fixtures covering edge cases observed during baseline measurement.

- SLI name: provision_to_ready_time

- Population: nodes in Dedicated and shared fleets that emit readiness events (pool_type tag)

title: Add Private observability smoke-test harness (kind + Helm) and gate on core signals
preview:
Motivation: We keep finding regressions in Private installs where Prometheus scrapes succeed but dashboards/rules don’t render (missing ConfigMaps), or metrics rename breaks the minimum pack. This PR adds a deterministic smoke-test harness that deploys a minimal Private stack into a fresh kind cluster and asserts the observability minimum pack is actually usable (scrapes + basic PromQL + dashboards + alert rules). This is intended to be the v1 gate for ENG-19156 and to prevent repeats of INT-11804 failures and SUP-49210-style issues.

What this adds: (1) `hack/private_observability_smoke/` harness that creates a kind cluster, installs dependencies (kube-prometheus-stack + grafana), installs Redwood Private via Helm (control-plane + gateway + one runtime deployment), then validates signals; (2) a Go-based validator binary that queries Prometheus HTTP API and checks for presence of expected time series and non-empty results; (3) a CI job that runs on PRs touching Helm/installer/metrics/observability codepaths; (4) documentation in-repo for running locally with tunables for air-gapped-ish constraints (image overrides, no external chart repos).

Validation scope (minimum supported): - Prometheus target discovery: Redwood pods appear in `/api/v1/targets` and are `up==1` - Golden signals: `redwood_request_total`, `redwood_request_errors_total`, and `redwood_request_latency_ms_bucket` (or recording-rule equivalents) are present and queryable - Runtime health signals: GPU metrics are not required in kind; we validate runtime queue depth + token throughput metrics if exported, but do not fail if GPU exporter is disabled - Dashboards/rules packaging: expected Grafana dashboard ConfigMaps exist and are labeled for the sidecar; PrometheusRule resources exist and load without parse errors.

Implementation notes: - Harness is idempotent and cleans up the kind cluster on exit; also supports `--keep-cluster` for debugging. - We pin kind node image and chart versions to reduce flake. - We avoid relying on external DNS by using port-forward to Prometheus/Grafana and querying over localhost. - Default timeouts were increased (cold start + image pulls) and we added bounded retries around target readiness.

Example validator checks (illustrative): `GET http://127.0.0.1:9090/api/v1/query?query=sum(rate(redwood_request_total[2m]))` must return a non-empty vector after generating a small amount of traffic against the gateway. We generate traffic via a minimal in-cluster job that hits `/v1/chat/completions` with a tiny prompt and expects HTTP 200.

Out of scope: full e2e quality evals, performance baselines, GPU exporter validation, and customer-specific sinks (Splunk/ELK). This is a smoke test for “signals show up” and “packaging isn’t broken.”

Follow-ups: - Add a second lane that runs with network policies enabled to catch scrape regressions earlier (related to incident thread about tightened NetworkPolicies). - Add a nightly job with a larger retention window to catch TSDB churn/memory regressions.

Checklist: - [x] New harness runnable locally (README) - [x] CI job added + scoped path filters - [x] Flake mitigation (pinned versions, retries, cleanup) - [x] Runbook link placeholders in alert annotations are not validated here (handled in observability-pack PRs) - [x] No secrets or payload content logged by harness (only status codes + request ids).

title: Add structured logging fields + redaction safeguards for Private troubleshooting
preview:
Title: Add structured logging fields + redaction safeguards for Private troubleshooting

Context
Private customers and Support/SRE need a reliable way to correlate failures across the gateway, control plane, and serving runtime without requiring payload logging or environment-specific bespoke parsing. Today, several components log different key names (or omit them entirely), and a few code paths still allow unredacted headers to slip into debug logs.

This PR aligns the log schema for Private installs with the minimum pack expectations and adds redaction safeguards suitable for regulated environments.

Summary of changes
1) Standardized structured fields (JSON logs)
- request_id: propagated from API gateway to downstream control-plane calls and runtime request handling. If an inbound X-Request-Id is present, we honor it (bounded length + charset validation); otherwise generate a UUIDv4.
- tenant_id_hash: stable per-cluster HMAC-SHA256 of tenant_id using a Private-only secret key (redwood-logging/tenant-hmac-key). This allows correlation across services without exposing raw tenant identifiers.
- route: normalized route identifier (route_id if available; otherwise a sanitized route name). Avoids high-cardinality raw path logging.
- model: normalized model identifier (model_id / registry handle) instead of raw model display strings.
- Additional alignment fields where available: component, service, environment, cluster_id, namespace, pod, node, version, build_sha, trace_id (if present), span_id (if present).

2) Redaction + safe defaults
- Added a central redaction utility used by gateway, policy/routing, and runtime request logging.
- Default redactions:
  - Headers: Authorization, Cookie, Set-Cookie, X-Api-Key, X-Redwood-Api-Key, X-Amz-Security-Token, X-Google-* access tokens
  - Query params: api_key, token, access_token, refresh_token
  - Body/payload: request prompt/messages are not logged (even at debug). We only log prompt_bytes (length), max_tokens, and sampling flags.
- Added guardrails so "log_request_body" style flags in legacy configs do not enable payload logging in Private builds; instead they emit a warning and remain disabled.

3) Cardinality controls
- model and route are truncated and normalized to a bounded set of characters.
- Removed a few ad-hoc fields that were previously logging full URLs and raw endpoint paths.
- Ensured tenant_id_hash is constant-size and does not create unbounded labels/fields.

4) Backwards compatibility
- Kept legacy keys (req_id, tenant) for one release cycle with a deprecation warning in logs.
- Introduced log_schema_version: "2025-08-structured-v1" for downstream collectors/parsers.

5) Tests + docs
- Added unit tests for redaction (headers/query params/body-like fields) and request_id propagation.
- Updated Private troubleshooting docs to reference the new fields and recommended queries in Loki/ELK/Splunk.

Example (runtime)
{"ts":"2025-08-11T18:42:15.219Z","level":"info","service":"runtime","component":"inference","log_schema_version":"2025-08-structured-v1","request_id":"c16d7fd8-5f7d-4f9e-8b9e-8b835f8ac3ad","tenant_id_hash":"hmac256:1d2d...a91f","route":"route:chat-completions","model":"model:llama3.1-70b-instruct@2025-07-29","status":"ok","latency_ms":183,"prompt_bytes":842,"max_tokens":256,"token_out":211,"gpu":"A100","pod":"runtime-7c6b8c7b7d-qk9h2"}

Why HMAC for tenant_id_hash?
- A plain hash can be vulnerable to offline enumeration if tenant IDs are low-entropy. Using an HMAC with a per-cluster key prevents that while still allowing stable correlation.

Operational notes
- New secret: redwood-logging/tenant-hmac-key is created automatically by the installer if not present. Rotation is supported but will break cross-time correlation for tenant_id_hash; we document this for customers.
- For air-gapped installs, no new images are introduced; only code/config changes.

Rollout / risk
- Low runtime risk: schema changes only, no request-path changes.
- Some downstream log filters might rely on legacy keys; we keep aliases for one release.

Checklist
- [x] Security review for redaction rules (Marcus Lin / Kevin Osei)
- [x] Added tests for request_id propagation and redaction
- [x] Updated documentation for field names + example queries
- [x] Verified no prompts/messages are logged (search in runtime + gateway code paths)

Related
- ENG-19149 (Private log pipeline default collector config)
- ENG-19157 (Security review: log redaction and retention)
- SUP-49277 (Splunk HEC 401: improved request_id correlation made this easier to debug; doc updates include HEC field mapping)

Notes for Support/SRE
Recommended primary key for correlation is request_id. tenant_id_hash should be used only for aggregation and multi-request analysis; do not attempt to reverse it (not possible without the cluster key).

title: Standardize metric labels and reduce cardinality for Private installs
preview: Context: Private installs run customer-managed Prometheus with tighter TSDB/storage limits and stricter cardinality constraints than our hosted environments. We observed high series churn from per-request and per-route/model labels, especially when customers have many routes, frequently changing model versions, or multi-tenant traffic. This PR standardizes label names across control plane + data plane services and introduces bounded label strategies for Private (while keeping a compatibility path for existing dashboards/alerts). Scope: (1) Label normalization: unify common label keys across services (service/component/namespace/cluster), rename inconsistent keys (route vs endpoint, model vs model_name), and remove labels that should never exist on metrics (request_id, raw path). (2) Cardinality reduction for Private: replace unbounded labels with bounded dimensions: route_group (config-defined, limited set), model_family (derived, bounded), tenant_bucket (optional, hashed bucket count), and status_class (2xx/4xx/5xx). (3) Compatibility: dual-emit old + new metrics/labels for a deprecation window behind a feature flag, plus recording rules that map old queries to new series so dashboards/alerts keep working. (4) Documentation: add guidance in the Private metrics standards doc and inline comments explaining why certain labels are disallowed. Implementation details: - Added a shared metrics helper package used by gateway, policy/routing, and runtime sidecars to enforce label allowlists and cap label values. - Introduced `REDWOOD_PRIVATE_METRICS_MODE=bounded` (default on for Private Helm values; off by default in hosted/dev) to enable bounding behavior. - For route dimension: emit `route_group` (from route config; defaults to "default"; max 50 distinct values per cluster enforced with "other" overflow). - For model dimension: emit `model_family` (e.g., llama3, mistral, qwen; unknown -> "other") and `model_variant` (optional; capped to top-N by request volume per process; overflow -> "other"). - For tenant dimension: do not emit raw tenant_id; emit `tenant_bucket` only when enabled (default disabled) to support coarse multi-tenant skew debugging without exploding series. - Removed `path` label usage from HTTP metrics in gateway; replaced with `route_group` + `handler` (bounded). - Updated recording rules used by the minimum pack dashboards to prefer the new labels, while still supporting legacy selectors. Notes: This is a stepping stone for the Private observability minimum pack (dashboards/alerts). Keeping dual emission avoids a hard cutover, but we should schedule removal once the minimum pack GA branch is stable. Testing: - Unit tests assert label allowlist enforcement and overflow behavior. - Added a micro-benchmark for the label bounding map to ensure we don’t introduce hot-path locks. - Verified in the private smoke-test harness that series count stays within expected limits on a synthetic 200-route / 80-model workload. Rollout plan: - Enable bounded mode by default for Private Helm chart values (separate PR in charts). - Keep legacy labels available for 2 releases; emit a log warning in bounded mode when legacy selectors are detected (dashboard queries) only in debug builds (no customer-facing noise). - Document migration in `docs/private/observability/metrics-migrations.md` and link in alert annotations where relevant.

(3) Compatibility: dual-emit old + new metrics/labels for a deprecation window behind a feature flag, plus recording rules that map old queries to new series so dashboards/alerts keep working. (4) Documentation: add guidance in the Private metrics standards doc and inline comments explaining why certain labels are disallowed. Implementation details: - Added a shared metrics helper package used by gateway, policy/routing, and runtime sidecars to enforce label allowlists and cap label values. - Introduced `REDWOOD_PRIVATE_METRICS_MODE=bounded` (default on for Private Helm values; off by default in hosted/dev) to enable bounding behavior. - For route dimension: emit `route_group` (from route config; defaults to "default"; max 50 distinct values per cluster enforced with "other" overflow). - For model dimension: emit `model_family` (e.g., llama3, mistral, qwen; unknown -> "other") and `model_variant` (optional; capped to top-N by request volume per process; overflow -> "other"). - For tenant dimension: do not emit raw tenant_id; emit `tenant_bucket` only when enabled (default disabled) to support coarse multi-tenant skew debugging without exploding series. - Removed `path` label usage from HTTP metrics in gateway; replaced with `route_group` + `handler` (bounded). - Updated recording rules used by the minimum pack dashboards to prefer the new labels, while still supporting legacy selectors. Notes: This is a stepping stone for the Private observability minimum pack (dashboards/alerts). Keeping dual emission avoids a hard cutover, but we should schedule removal once the minimum pack GA branch is stable. Testing: - Unit tests assert label allowlist enforcement and overflow behavior. - Added a micro-benchmark for the label bounding map to ensure we don’t introduce hot-path locks. - Verified in the private smoke-test harness that series count stays within expected limits on a synthetic 200-route / 80-model workload. Rollout plan: - Enable bounded mode by default for Private Helm chart values (separate PR in charts). - Keep legacy labels available for 2 releases; emit a log warning in bounded mode when legacy selectors are detected (dashboard queries) only in debug builds (no customer-facing noise). - Document migration in `docs/private/observability/metrics-migrations.md` and link in alert annotations where relevant.

title: Add Private minimum pack Prometheus alert rules: latency + availability (with SLO burn recording rules)
preview:
Context: This PR introduces the baseline alerting + recording rules for the Redwood Private observability minimum pack, focused on the highest-impact failure modes for inference availability and latency regressions. These are intended to be safe-by-default in customer clusters (bounded cardinality, conservative paging) and to map cleanly to the SLO guidance we’ve been using in staging.

What’s included (v1):
- Recording rules (namespace: redwood_private:*) used by dashboards + alerts so queries are consistent and cheaper.
- Latency SLO burn alerts (multi-window, multi-burn) for the API gateway and runtime request path.
- Availability alerts (5xx rate + blackbox reachability) for API ingress and control plane critical services.
- Alert annotations include runbook + first steps links and a canonical dashboard deep link placeholder.
- promtool tests for rule syntax + basic evaluation fixtures.

Alert philosophy / thresholds (rationale):
- We page only on issues that are likely customer-visible and sustained.
- Latency: burn-rate alerts use a 2-window strategy (fast burn for rapid regressions, slow burn for creeping issues). This matches Aisha’s guidance for Private: prefer burn rates over raw p95 spikes to avoid noise in heterogeneous customer traffic.
- Availability: default is request-based (5xx ratio) rather than absolute error count; includes a minimum request rate gate to avoid low-traffic false positives.
- We intentionally avoid high-cardinality labels in alerts (no tenant_id, no raw model name, no full route). Where route-level is needed, we aggregate to route_group and/or service.

Defaults (tuned for min pack):
- Page (Sev2): sustained burn likely to exhaust error budget within 1h OR API is unreachable.
- Ticket (Sev3): slower burn / partial degradation / early warning.
- Warning alerts are created but set to disabled-by-default in values; the helm wiring PR will decide final toggles.

Runbooks:
- Latency burn: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPRIV/Runbook+-+Private+Latency+SLO+Burn
- Availability / 5xx: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPRIV/Runbook+-+Private+Availability+and+5xx
- Prometheus scrape health (support): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGINFRA/Prometheus+and+Grafana+for+Private+Installations

Notes / follow-ups:
- GPU saturation, OOM, and restart-loop alerts are tracked separately (ENG-19148).
- This PR does not wire rules into helm values; that is handled in redwood-helm-charts PR-18405.
- We should revisit the latency objective (currently defaults to 800ms p95 for chat endpoints) after we have 2–3 customer baselines from validation.

Checklist:
- [x] Recording rules included for RPS, error ratio, latency quantiles, and SLI measurements
- [x] Alerts include runbook URLs and concise "what to do first" steps
- [x] promtool rule tests added
- [x] Label cardinality bounded (no per-tenant or per-model alert dimension)
- [x] Reviewed against Private metrics/alerting standards

Screenshots/Examples:
- Included in the conversation: example firing of LatencySLOBurnFast after injected delay; and non-firing for low traffic due to request rate gate.

title: Add Private minimum pack: versioned Grafana dashboard bundle (runtime + control plane + infra)
preview: Summary: This PR adds the first versioned Grafana dashboard bundle for the Private Observability Minimum Pack. The dashboards are designed to be shipped as immutable artifacts (checked into this repo) and later installed via `redwood-helm-charts`/`redwood-private-installer` (follow-ups). Scope: 1) Runtime overview dashboard focused on golden signals + inference-specific panels (token throughput, batching, KV cache, GPU utilization). 2) Control plane health dashboard (gateway/auth/policy/config services) with standard service golden signals and dependency panels (DB/redis if present). 3) Infra dashboard for customer cluster basics required to triage most common Private incidents (node pressure, PVC pressure, pod restart loops, scrape status). Design notes / constraints: - Private environments require bounded label cardinality; dashboards avoid templating on high-cardinality labels like raw `route`, `tenant_id`, `request_id`. Where filtering is useful, panels use a small set of safe dimensions (`namespace`, `service`, `cluster`, `instance`), and model/route usage is represented via top-k panels based on recording rules (to be introduced in ENG-19144/PR-18356). - Dashboards are versioned under `dashboards/private/minimum-pack/v1.0.0/` and include stable UIDs prefixed with `rw-private-` to ensure upgrades don’t create duplicates. - Panels include conservative defaults (5m/1h ranges) and annotate ‘expected exporters’ (kube-state-metrics, node-exporter, NVIDIA DCGM exporter) but do not hard-depend on optional exporters (panels degrade gracefully when metrics are missing). What’s included (v1.0.0): - `Runtime / Overview (Private)` (UID: `rw-private-runtime-overview`) - `Control Plane / Health (Private)` (UID: `rw-private-control-plane-health`) - `Private / Cluster Infra` (UID: `rw-private-cluster-infra`) - `Private / Prometheus Scrape Health` (UID: `rw-private-prom-scrape-health`) (small helper dashboard to debug ‘empty panels’ issues) Implementation details: - Adds a bundle manifest (`dashboards/private/minimum-pack/bundle.yaml`) that enumerates dashboards, UIDs, and target folder names for the installer. - Adds lightweight validation tooling in CI (`make dashboards-validate`) to ensure exported JSON is stable, has a UID, and does not embed org-specific datasource IDs. - Standardizes dashboard tags: `redwood`, `private`, `minimum-pack`, and component tag (e.g. `runtime`, `control-plane`, `infra`). Testing: - Ran `make dashboards-validate` locally. - Imported into Grafana 10.4 with a local Prometheus, verified template variables resolve and panels render with a simulated dataset. - Verified dashboards import with no ‘Datasource not found’ errors by using named datasource `Prometheus` and `Loki` placeholders (installer will map). Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Wire into `redwood-helm-charts` values + installer packaging (ENG-19152 / PR-18405). - Add recording rules and alerts referenced by some panels (ENG-19144 / PR-18356). - Add on-prem offline bundle inclusion + checksums/signing (ENG-19154 / PR-18441).

title: Add alert rules + routing for Dedicated max-bound saturation and sustained queue buildup
preview:
Title: Add alert rules + routing for Dedicated max-bound saturation and sustained queue buildup

Context
Dedicated autoscaling bounds are rolling out to pilot customers. During pilot we’ve seen two recurring failure/impact modes that are easy to miss without dedicated alerting:
1) Deployment hits max bound for an extended period while demand continues increasing (customers see queueing / p95 latency regressions; support tickets like SUP-28411).
2) Sustained queue buildup (even if not max-bound) indicating a scaling issue, forecast signal outage, or model-level throughput regression.

This PR adds:
- Prometheus alert rules for max-bound saturation and sustained queue buildup.
- Alertmanager routing to Dedicated on-call (page) vs #eng-infra (ticket/notify) depending on severity and duration.
- Rule annotations/runbook links aligned with the Dedicated autoscaler troubleshooting runbook.

What’s included
- New alerts (names follow metric naming standards and keep labels low-cardinality):
  - DedicatedMaxBoundSustained (page): bounded at max AND queue depth elevated for >= 10m.
  - DedicatedMaxBoundHitRateHigh (notify): high ratio of bounded_desired == max over a rolling window, even if queue is not yet elevated.
  - DedicatedQueueDepthSustained (page): queue depth above threshold for >= 15m (guarded by traffic floor to avoid noise).
  - DedicatedMinBoundUnmet (notify): min bound cannot be met (region capacity shortage / provisioning failure), intended as early warning to SRE + capacity planning.
- Routing:
  - Severity=page routes to PagerDuty service "dedicated-oncall" with grouping by (deployment_id, region).
  - Severity=notify routes to Slack #eng-infra and creates a ticket via existing webhook (no new integration).
- Documentation pointers:
  - Annotations include dashboard links (from pr-1934) and runbook section anchors.

Thresholds (initial)
- Queue depth signal uses dedicated_autoscaler_queue_depth (already emitted by fleet telemetry) with a traffic floor via req_rate to avoid paging on low-volume deployments.
- Max-bound saturation uses dedicated_autoscaler_bounded_desired_replicas == dedicated_autoscaler_max_replicas AND queue depth above a small baseline (to avoid false positives when customers intentionally cap above demand).
- All thresholds were chosen to match pilot expectations and can be tuned post-canary; see inline comments in rules.

Files / areas changed
- alerts/dedicated/autoscaling_bounds_alerts.yaml: new rule group(s) for bounds + queueing.
- alertmanager/routes/dedicated.yaml: new routes + grouping rules.
- alertmanager/receivers/pagerduty.yaml: ensure dedicated-oncall receiver exists and has correct service key reference.
- dashboards/links.yaml: adds canonical URLs used by alert annotations.
- tests/promtool_rules_test.yaml: rule unit tests covering basic firing/non-firing cases.

Rollout plan
- Landing behind existing Dedicated bounds rollout (feature is opt-in per account), but alerting is safe to enable globally since it only evaluates when metrics exist.
- We will initially enable paging routes only for pilot accounts (via matcher on customer_tier=pilot) for 1 week, then broaden to all Dedicated once noise is acceptable.

Checklist
- [x] promtool check passes
- [x] alert rule tests added
- [x] annotations include runbook + dashboard
- [x] routing reviewed by SRE

Notes / follow-ups
- We should consider adding a separate alert for "predictive signal stale" once the predictor staleness metric is stable (tracked in ENG-22142).
- The min-bound unmet alert is notify-only by design; paging policy should be revisited after we collect baseline rate.

Screenshots
N/A (rule changes only).

title: Add canary scenarios for predictive autoscaling (traffic ramps + periodic spikes)
preview:
## Summary
Adds two new perf-canary workloads intended to catch latency regressions and oscillation when Dedicated predictive autoscaling is enabled:
1) `predictive_ramp_30m`: steady baseline then a monotonic ramp (to mimic “daily ramp” customers).
2) `predictive_spikes_10m`: periodic spikes with short recovery windows (to mimic bursty SaaS/cron-driven traffic).

These scenarios run against a Dedicated deployment with predictive mode toggled via canary config (feature-flagged per account) and record:
- p50/p95/p99 end-to-end latency
- queue depth (when available)
- autoscaler desired vs bounded desired replicas (to detect max-bound saturation and clamp behavior)
- scale event reasons + step sizes (to detect oscillation)
- prediction freshness + error (if forecast provider is enabled in the canary env)

The goal is to gate pilot expansions: predictive mode should not regress p95 during known ramps and should not introduce high-frequency scale-up/scale-down cycles under periodic spikes.

## Why
During the pilot we saw a case where predictive inputs + noisy saturation signals caused capacity jitter and increased latency during a customer ramp (ref: incident thread + follow-up hotfix in main autoscaler). This PR adds repeatable canary coverage so we can catch:
- predictor returning null/stale values leading to unsafe downscale (should fall back to reactive)
- oscillation due to overly-aggressive forecast deltas
- max-bound clamping causing sustained queue buildup (signal for misconfigured max or insufficient fleet capacity)

## What changed
- Added scenario definitions and traffic curves:
  - `scenarios/predictive_ramp_30m.yaml`
  - `scenarios/predictive_spikes_10m.yaml`
- Added thresholds + pass/fail gating:
  - p95 latency must remain within +10% of the reactive baseline run (same model + same request mix)
  - scale event frequency: no more than 6 scale actions / 10 min window after initial ramp stabilization
  - bounded-by-max detection: if `bounded_desired == max` for >5m AND queue depth > threshold => fail (signals capacity mismatch)
  - predictor staleness: if `forecast_age_seconds > 300` => mark predictive metrics as degraded and require reactive fallback tag
- Extended result emitter to include autoscaling “reason” tags in the summary artifacts (so we can tell “predictive_up” vs “reactive_saturation”).
- Added README notes for running locally against staging (requires canary service account with Dedicated read permissions).

## How to run
- CI: runs nightly and on-demand via `workflow_dispatch` (see `.github/workflows/predictive-autoscaling-canary.yml`).
- Local:
  - `CANARY_TARGET=dedicated-staging-us-west-2 CANARY_SCENARIO=predictive_ramp_30m make run`
  - `CANARY_TARGET=dedicated-staging-us-west-2 CANARY_SCENARIO=predictive_spikes_10m make run`

## Risk / rollout
Low risk: changes are isolated to perf-canary. The only operational coupling is metric name usage; this PR uses existing metric names emitted by the autoscaler and treats missing metrics as “skip” (non-fatal) to avoid breaking canary in regions that haven’t rolled out the new metrics yet.

## Checklist
- [x] Added scenario docs + example outputs
- [x] Verified canary runner handles missing prediction provider (marks run as reactive-only)
- [x] Validated thresholds on two replay runs against staging (reactive vs predictive) with no failures
- [x] Added artifact links in the workflow summary (Grafana + JSON summary)

## Example output (from staging)
- `predictive_ramp_30m`: p95 +3.2% vs reactive baseline; 3 scale actions after stabilization; no bound-hit.
- `predictive_spikes_10m`: p95 +5.8% vs reactive baseline; 5 scale actions; one brief clamp (<60s) without queue growth.

## Review notes / conversation
Rafael Mendes (SRE): Can we make the oscillation threshold model-aware? Some models have noisier token times and may drive more scale events.
Leila Farouk (author): Good call. For now this is pinned to the pilot model set in staging (same as Dedicated pilot). I added a config knob `max_scale_actions_per_10m` in the scenario YAML so we can tune per model when we expand coverage.

Vanessa Ortiz (Fleet): Please ensure clamp failures are actionable; we don’t want canary to fail just because max bound is intentionally low.
Leila Farouk (author): Updated clamp gating: it only fails when (a) clamped at max for >5m AND (b) queue depth exceeds the threshold (sustained queueing). If queue depth is unavailable, it logs a warning and does not hard-fail.

Sana Farid (Forecast eval): Can we record forecast freshness explicitly? We’ve had stale forecasts cause misleading results.
Leila Farouk (author): Added `forecast_age_seconds` and a “predictive_degraded” tag if age >300s. Also added a check that requires the runner to observe `reactive_fallback=true` if predictive is degraded.

Samantha Roy (Telemetry): Metric label cardinality—avoid adding customer_id/deployment_id to perf-canary summary.
Leila Farouk (author): Confirmed. The runner stores IDs only in the private run metadata artifact (restricted) and uses hashed identifiers in the public summary.

## CI
- canary-lint: pass
- unit (runner): pass
- scenario-validate: pass
- dry-run (no traffic): pass

## Merge
Squash merge into `main`.

Refs: ENG-22147 (load test + simulation), ENG-22154 (game day), ENG-22142 (forecast ingestion integration).

- [x] Added scenario docs + example outputs
- [x] Verified canary runner handles missing prediction provider (marks run as reactive-only)
- [x] Validated thresholds on two replay runs against staging (reactive vs predictive) with no failures
- [x] Added artifact links in the workflow summary (Grafana + JSON summary)

## Example output (from staging)
- `predictive_ramp_30m`: p95 +3.2% vs reactive baseline; 3 scale actions after stabilization; no bound-hit.
- `predictive_spikes_10m`: p95 +5.8% vs reactive baseline; 5 scale actions; one brief clamp (<60s) without queue growth.

## Review notes / conversation
Rafael Mendes (SRE): Can we make the oscillation threshold model-aware? Some models have noisier token times and may drive more scale events.
Leila Farouk (author): Good call. For now this is pinned to the pilot model set in staging (same as Dedicated pilot). I added a config knob `max_scale_actions_per_10m` in the scenario YAML so we can tune per model when we expand coverage.

Vanessa Ortiz (Fleet): Please ensure clamp failures are actionable; we don’t want canary to fail just because max bound is intentionally low.
Leila Farouk (author): Updated clamp gating: it only fails when (a) clamped at max for >5m AND (b) queue depth exceeds the threshold (sustained queueing). If queue depth is unavailable, it logs a warning and does not hard-fail.

Sana Farid (Forecast eval): Can we record forecast freshness explicitly? We’ve had stale forecasts cause misleading results.
Leila Farouk (author): Added `forecast_age_seconds` and a “predictive_degraded” tag if age >300s. Also added a check that requires the runner to observe `reactive_fallback=true` if predictive is degraded.

Samantha Roy (Telemetry): Metric label cardinality—avoid adding customer_id/deployment_id to perf-canary summary.
Leila Farouk (author): Confirmed. The runner stores IDs only in the private run metadata artifact (restricted) and uses hashed identifiers in the public summary.

## CI
- canary-lint: pass
- unit (runner): pass
- scenario-validate: pass
- dry-run (no traffic): pass

## Merge
Squash merge into `main`.

Refs: ENG-22147 (load test + simulation), ENG-22154 (game day), ENG-22142 (forecast ingestion integration).

title: Dedicated autoscaler: add metrics + structured logs for desired vs bounded capacity and prediction error
preview:
Context: As part of Dedicated autoscaling bounds + predictive mode, we need auditability and operability around what the autoscaler *wanted* to do vs what it actually did after applying customer bounds (min/max) and safety invariants. This PR adds first-class metrics and structured logs for (1) requested desired replicas vs bounded desired replicas, (2) bound-hit counters (clamped to min/max), (3) prediction error and staleness when predictive mode is enabled, and (4) an explicit scale-event reason enum that shows up in both metrics and logs.

What’s in this PR:
- New gauge metrics (emitted every autoscaler tick per deployment/region):
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.desired_replicas (the raw desired output from the decision function)
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.bounded_desired_replicas (after applying min/max + safety floors)
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.current_replicas
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.queue_depth (existing signal re-emitted consistently for dashboards)
- New counters:
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.bound_hit_total{bound="min"|"max"} increments when desired is clamped
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.scale_event_total{direction="up"|"down", reason=*} increments on actual scale actions
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.prediction_stale_total increments when a forecast is ignored due to staleness
- New histograms (guarded by config to avoid extra cardinality):
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.prediction_error_ratio (|predicted - actual| / max(actual,1))
  - redwood.dedicated.autoscaler.decision_latency_ms (internal time to compute desired)
- Structured decision logs (one per tick) with stable field names for searchability and compliance reviews. Key fields:
  - deployment_id, customer_id, region
  - policy_min, policy_max, predictive_enabled
  - desired_replicas, bounded_desired_replicas, current_replicas
  - scale_action (none|up|down), scale_delta
  - reason (enum), reason_detail (short string)
  - predictor_used (true/false), predictor_age_seconds

Scale reason enum (used consistently across metric label + log field):
- reactive_queue_depth
- reactive_saturation
- predictive_forecast
- bounded_by_max
- bounded_by_min
- cooldown_active
- no_change
- invalid_prediction

Cardinality / labeling:
- All metrics are labeled with deployment_id and region. customer_id is included only in structured logs (NOT as a metric label) to avoid high-cardinality blowups. This matches the current fleet metrics policy.
- reason labels are limited to the enum above (no freeform strings).

Illustrative example (structured log):
{"msg":"autoscaler_tick","deployment_id":"ded-9d2f...","region":"us-east-1","policy_min":4,"policy_max":24,"predictive_enabled":true,"desired_replicas":31,"bounded_desired_replicas":24,"current_replicas":20,"scale_action":"up","scale_delta":4,"reason":"bounded_by_max","reason_detail":"desired=31 clamped to max=24","predictor_used":true,"predictor_age_seconds":58}

Tests:
- Unit tests for reason selection (predictive vs reactive overrides) to ensure we don’t emit conflicting reason labels.
- Integration-style test for clamp behavior to ensure bound_hit_total increments exactly once per tick when clamping occurs.

Follow-ups (out of this PR):
- Grafana dashboard panels + alert rules live in observability-pack PRs.
- Control plane audit event schema (who/when/what) is handled in the audit-events PR.

Rollout:
- Metrics are behind the existing dedicated_autoscaling_bounds feature gate. When disabled, no new bounded/predictive metrics are emitted.
- Prediction histograms default off in prod until we validate cost/volume during pilot (config: autoscaler.emit_prediction_histograms=false).

Refs: ENG-22143, ENG-22140. Also informs debugging for SUP-28479 (pilot oscillation) by exposing scale_event reasons and predictor staleness.
