title: Private minimum pack: add GPU saturation + OOM/resource pressure alerts for inference pods
preview:
Context: Private minimum pack needs a minimal, supported set of alerts that catch the highest-impact inference failure modes (GPU saturation, GPU memory pressure, OOM kills, restart loops) without overloading customer on-calls. This PR adds GPU/resource pressure alert rules scoped to Redwood inference pods and wires them into the existing private rules bundle. It includes tuning notes from the alert-noise review and enforces label/cardinality constraints for customer Prometheus.

What’s included: (1) GPU saturation alerts (warning/ticket): sustained high GPU utilization on inference pods aggregated by cluster/namespace/workload; (2) GPU memory pressure (warning): high framebuffer utilization (FB used/total) using DCGM metrics when available; (3) OOM kill alerts (critical/page): repeated OOMKilled terminations for redwood-runtime containers over a short window; (4) restart loop alerts (warning -> critical escalation): excessive restarts for runtime pods; (5) node/pod resource pressure signals (warning): Kubernetes NodeMemoryPressure for nodes running runtime pods, and container memory RSS high (guardrail) where kubelet/cadvisor metrics exist.

Noise tuning / defaults: A few alerts are intentionally shipped disabled-by-default via a `privateMinimumPack.alerts.gpu.enableExperimental` gate in values (see helm wiring in follow-up PRs) because clusters differ significantly in baseline utilization. In particular, the "HighGPUUtilization" warning threshold is conservative and requires a long `for:` to avoid flapping during bursty token workloads. The OOMKilled alert remains enabled by default (impact is high and signal is reliable). All alerts include runbook annotations with first-actions oriented toward Private support/SRE.

Metric dependencies: This assumes (a) kube-state-metrics is present for pod termination reasons and restarts, (b) node-exporter/kubelet metrics for node conditions and container memory, and (c) DCGM exporter for GPU metrics. If DCGM metrics are absent, GPU-specific alerts will stay inactive; the rules are written to avoid hard-failing eval.

Cardinality constraints: Alerts intentionally avoid `model`, `route`, `tenant`, and `request_id` labels. Aggregations are by `cluster`, `namespace`, and `workload`/`deployment` only. For DCGM, we aggregate across GPUs per node to avoid per-GPU paging; detailed per-GPU dashboards remain the place to drill in.

Testing: Validated rules load in Prometheus (promtool), unit tests cover common label sets, and smoke-test harness was run in staging-private with synthetic load + forced OOM scenarios. Included one follow-up note: customers running strict NetworkPolicies may need to allow DCGM scrape targets; that is called out in the runbook.

Follow-ups: (1) Wire `enableExperimental` toggles into `redwood-helm-charts` private values (tracked in ENG-19152); (2) Add a short troubleshooting section to the Day-2 runbook for "DCGM metrics missing" and "OOMKilled alert firing" patterns; (3) Consider a recording rule for GPU mem fraction to simplify dashboards if we keep these long-term.

Alert summary (names may change slightly before GA): - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeGPUUtilizationHigh (warning, disabled-by-default) - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeGPUMemoryPressure (warning, disabled-by-default) - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeOOMKilled (critical, enabled) - RedwoodPrivateRuntimeRestartLoop (warning/critical, enabled) - RedwoodPrivateNodeMemoryPressureForRuntime (warning, enabled)

Notes for reviewers: Please focus on (a) severity mapping (page vs ticket), (b) label usage/cardinality, (c) thresholds and `for:` durations, and (d) runbook annotation clarity (what to do first).

title: Add Private upgrade health dashboards (prechecks, migrations, error rates, rollbacks)
preview: Summary: Adds a Grafana dashboard pack for Redwood Private upgrade health, focused on the new installer-driven upgrade workflow (prechecks -> backup -> control plane rollout -> data plane rollout -> migrations -> postchecks, with rollback entry points). Dashboard set covers (1) precheck failure rates + breakdown by check_id/severity, (2) DB migration durations and timeouts/lock waits, (3) API/control-plane error rates during upgrade windows, and (4) rollback frequency + outcomes. Motivation: SRE and Release Eng asked for a single place to validate upgrade readiness and spot regressions during beta rollouts; today we rely on scattered logs and ad-hoc queries. This PR makes upgrades observable with consistent panels and variables that work in Hosted VPC Private and on-prem (including air-gapped) where only Prometheus + Loki are available. What’s included: - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Overview" (upgrade success rate, active upgrades, error budget impact proxy, rollbacks). - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Prechecks" (fail rate, top failing checks, blocking vs warning, remediation link rendering). - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Migrations" (p50/p95 duration by migration_name, lock wait time, failed migrations, timeouts). - New dashboard: "Private Upgrades / Upgrade Window Errors" (HTTP 5xx/429, gRPC error codes if present, control-plane reachability, installer command failure count). - Optional annotation integration: upgrade window markers derived from audit/installer events when present; otherwise uses a manual label/annotation query. Metrics/log sources: - Prometheus metrics emitted by installer/controller-plane components: redwood_private_upgrade_runs_total, redwood_private_upgrade_precheck_results_total{check_id,severity,result}, redwood_private_upgrade_stage_duration_seconds_bucket{stage}, redwood_db_migration_duration_seconds_bucket{migration}, redwood_db_migration_failures_total{migration,reason}, redwood_private_rollback_runs_total{result}. - Loki log-derived counters for older installs missing a subset of metrics (best-effort fallback queries). Installation notes: Dashboards are templated with common labels (cluster, environment, customer, deployment_mode). On-prem guidance included: set prometheus external_labels for customer + cluster; if unavailable, dashboards still work but variables are limited. Screenshots: attached in PR (Overview + Prechecks + Migrations). Risks/rollout: purely additive dashboards/queries; no alert changes in this PR. Follow-ups: add alerts for migration timeout and elevated rollback frequency once beta thresholds stabilize; wire in audit event fields after redwood/pr-29187 lands everywhere.

title: Annotate upgrade-window incidents + link to Private release artifacts
preview:
## Summary
When a Private upgrade is running (VPC or on-prem), we want incidents that fire in that window to be clearly labeled and automatically correlated to the exact release artifacts involved.

This PR updates incident-bot’s enrichment pipeline to:
- detect upgrade windows based on a canonical source (release train + installer upgrade state signals)
- tag incidents created during those windows as upgrade-related
- attach links to relevant release artifacts (release tag, build provenance, and installer plan bundle) so on-call can quickly determine whether a spike is likely upgrade-induced vs. product regression.

This is part of the "Private upgrade + rollback mechanism" workstream focused on auditability and reduced MTTR.

## Motivation
We’ve repeatedly had to manually correlate incidents during maintenance windows:
- Was there an upgrade running?
- Which Private version / chart bundle / installer build?
- Was the incident before/after DB migrations?

This PR makes that correlation automatic and consistent, and produces a trail that Support can paste into customer comms (esp. regulated customers).

## What changed
### 1) Upgrade window detection
We enrich incidents with `upgrade_window` metadata when *either* of the following are true:
- an upgrade window annotation exists in the release calendar (time-bounded)
- an installer upgrade-state heartbeat indicates an upgrade is in progress for the environment

Precedence:
1. installer upgrade-state heartbeat (most accurate, environment-specific)
2. release calendar window (fallback; useful when signals are delayed)

### 2) Artifact linking
If we can resolve a Private release version, we attach:
- `private_release_version` (e.g. `private-2025.05.1`)
- `release_artifact_url` (release page / provenance)
- `installer_bundle_url` (where the installer fetched the upgrade plan + checksums)

If we can resolve an upgrade plan, we attach:
- `upgrade_plan_id`
- `upgrade_plan_sha256`

### 3) Tag + annotation format (stable keys)
New normalized fields on the incident annotation:
- `upgrade_window:true|false`
- `upgrade_source:installer|calendar|unknown`
- `upgrade_phase:prechecks|backup|control_plane|data_plane|migrations|rollback|unknown` (best effort)

### 4) UI-friendly output
Incident-bot posts a short, skimmable block in the incident channel:

> Upgrade context: ✅ upgrade window detected (source: installer)
> Version: private-2025.05.1
> Plan: upgplan_01J9F3… (sha256: 7b9f…)
> Artifacts: release • bundle

## Implementation details
- Adds `UpgradeContextResolver` with two backends:
  - `InstallerStateBackend` (reads upgrade-state events emitted by the Private installer)
  - `ReleaseCalendarBackend` (reads the release train schedule exported to the annotations store)
- Adds `ArtifactLinker` to generate canonical URLs (release tag + bundle path) from version/build metadata.
- Adds a small cache (TTL 60s) to avoid hammering the annotations store during bursty incident creation.

## Changed areas
- `src/enrichers/upgrade_context.ts` (new)
- `src/enrichers/index.ts` (wire into enrichment pipeline)
- `src/backends/installer_state.ts` (new)
- `src/backends/release_calendar.ts` (new)
- `src/artifacts/linker.ts` (new)
- `src/renderers/slack_blocks.ts` (adds upgrade context section)
- `test/upgrade_context.spec.ts` (new)
- `test/fixtures/installer_state_events.json` (new)
- `docs/runbooks/incident-annotations.md` (updated)

## Example payload
Before:
- `tags: ["private", "k8s", "error-rate"]`

After:
- `tags: ["private", "k8s", "error-rate", "upgrade_window"]`
- `annotations.upgrade_window = "true"`
- `annotations.private_release_version = "private-2025.05.1"`
- `annotations.release_artifact_url = "https://github.com/redwoodinference/redwood-private/releases/tag/private-2025.05.1"`
- `annotations.upgrade_plan_id = "upgplan_01J9F3K8Q..."`

## Tests
- Unit tests for resolver precedence + partial data scenarios
- Fixture-based tests for mapping installer phases -> normalized `upgrade_phase`
- Integration smoke test in CI: creates a synthetic incident event, ensures Slack message includes upgrade section when signals are present

## Rollout / risk
- Safe by default: if upgrade context cannot be resolved, no new tag is applied and the renderer omits the upgrade block.
- No changes to incident creation; enrichment is additive.

## Follow-ups
- Add Grafana annotation emit for upgrade windows (so dashboards visually align with incident-bot tags) (tracked in ENG-4899)
- Consider adding a "maintenance window" tag for non-upgrade events (certificate rotations, infra migrations)

## Related
- ENG-4899 (auditability: upgrade artifacts and logs)
- SUP-18421 (upgrade-related incidents during migrations)
- Private upgrade/rollback project: change-management + runbooks

---

### Commits
- incident-bot: add upgrade context resolver + artifact linker
- incident-bot: render upgrade context in Slack + normalize tags
- tests: add fixtures and precedence cases
- docs: document new annotation keys

### CI
✅ lint
✅ unit
✅ integration-smoke

---

## Review notes
**Rafael Mendes:** Can we ensure we don’t mis-tag incidents when calendar window is broad (e.g., customer extends maintenance)?

**Allison Grant (author):** Added resolver precedence + tightened calendar matching to require environment id + explicit window. Also added `upgrade_source` so we can audit false positives.

**Connor O'Brien:** Please ensure artifact URLs are stable across mirror endpoints (on-prem).

**Allison Grant (author):** ArtifactLinker now prefers canonical release tag URLs; bundle link is only attached when we have a resolvable bundle origin. For on-prem mirrors, we attach `installer_bundle_ref` (opaque) rather than guessing a URL.

**Sean Gallagher:** Add rate-limit/caching; this will run during bursts.

**Allison Grant (author):** Added TTL cache (60s) keyed by env id + window start.

**Dev Patel:** Please add a short blurb to the Support playbook about what to paste into customer updates.

**Allison Grant (author):** Updated docs/runbooks/incident-annotations.md with a Support snippet + field definitions.

Approvals:
- Rafael Mendes ✅
- Connor O'Brien ✅
- Sean Gallagher ✅

Merged via squash.

- `upgrade_plan_id`
- `upgrade_plan_sha256`

### 3) Tag + annotation format (stable keys)
New normalized fields on the incident annotation:
- `upgrade_window:true|false`
- `upgrade_source:installer|calendar|unknown`
- `upgrade_phase:prechecks|backup|control_plane|data_plane|migrations|rollback|unknown` (best effort)

### 4) UI-friendly output
Incident-bot posts a short, skimmable block in the incident channel:

> Upgrade context: ✅ upgrade window detected (source: installer)
> Version: private-2025.05.1
> Plan: upgplan_01J9F3… (sha256: 7b9f…)
> Artifacts: release • bundle

## Implementation details
- Adds `UpgradeContextResolver` with two backends:
  - `InstallerStateBackend` (reads upgrade-state events emitted by the Private installer)
  - `ReleaseCalendarBackend` (reads the release train schedule exported to the annotations store)
- Adds `ArtifactLinker` to generate canonical URLs (release tag + bundle path) from version/build metadata.
- Adds a small cache (TTL 60s) to avoid hammering the annotations store during bursty incident creation.

## Changed areas
- `src/enrichers/upgrade_context.ts` (new)
- `src/enrichers/index.ts` (wire into enrichment pipeline)
- `src/backends/installer_state.ts` (new)
- `src/backends/release_calendar.ts` (new)
- `src/artifacts/linker.ts` (new)
- `src/renderers/slack_blocks.ts` (adds upgrade context section)
- `test/upgrade_context.spec.ts` (new)
- `test/fixtures/installer_state_events.json` (new)
- `docs/runbooks/incident-annotations.md` (updated)

## Example payload
Before:
- `tags: ["private", "k8s", "error-rate"]`

After:
- `tags: ["private", "k8s", "error-rate", "upgrade_window"]`
- `annotations.upgrade_window = "true"`
- `annotations.private_release_version = "private-2025.05.1"`
- `annotations.release_artifact_url = "https://github.com/redwoodinference/redwood-private/releases/tag/private-2025.05.1"`
- `annotations.upgrade_plan_id = "upgplan_01J9F3K8Q..."`

## Tests
- Unit tests for resolver precedence + partial data scenarios
- Fixture-based tests for mapping installer phases -> normalized `upgrade_phase`
- Integration smoke test in CI: creates a synthetic incident event, ensures Slack message includes upgrade section when signals are present

## Rollout / risk
- Safe by default: if upgrade context cannot be resolved, no new tag is applied and the renderer omits the upgrade block.
- No changes to incident creation; enrichment is additive.

## Follow-ups
- Add Grafana annotation emit for upgrade windows (so dashboards visually align with incident-bot tags) (tracked in ENG-4899)
- Consider adding a "maintenance window" tag for non-upgrade events (certificate rotations, infra migrations)

## Related
- ENG-4899 (auditability: upgrade artifacts and logs)
- SUP-18421 (upgrade-related incidents during migrations)

---

### Commits

title: Add internal support debug endpoint to explain burst admission decisions (redacted)
preview:
## Summary
Adds an internal-only debug endpoint + lightweight CLI tooling to help Support/SRE explain *why* a Dedicated burst request was admitted/denied, with deterministic inputs captured and safe redaction.

This is intended to reduce back-and-forth on tickets like SUP-28411 (“denials higher than expected”) and SUP-28503 (“429s when burst cap reached”) by providing a structured, consistent explanation including: policy snapshot, computed limits, fairness state, relevant fleet safety gates, and the final denial reason code.

## What’s included
- **Internal endpoint (mTLS + RBAC only):** `POST /internal/v1/burst/admission/explain`
  - Accepts either a `request_id` (preferred) or a minimal request envelope (tenant + region + hw_class + model + tokens_requested + is_burst + timestamp).
  - Returns a redacted explanation object:
    - decision: admitted|denied|unknown
    - denial_reason_code (when denied)
    - evaluated_rules (names + pass/fail)
    - computed_limits (baseline, burst_cap, max_burst_ratio, cooldown)
    - fairness (token bucket state summary, not raw per-request history)
    - fleet_safety (pool saturation, global breaker state)
    - telemetry (age/lag buckets used by policy evaluation)
    - policy_snapshot_id + config_versions
- **Decision store hook:** best-effort lookup by `request_id` for the last N minutes (in-memory for now; same node that served the decision). If not found, returns `decision=unknown` with guidance.
- **Redaction:**
  - Never returns prompts, completions, customer headers, auth tokens, raw IPs.
  - Model name is returned; route is returned as a normalized route-id.
  - Tenant identifier is returned as `tenant_slug` only when caller has `support:tenant:read`; otherwise `tenant_hash`.
  - Request correlation ids are preserved.
  - Structured fields only (no raw log lines).
- **CLI for on-call / Support enablement:** `rw-support burst explain --request-id <id>`
  - Calls the internal endpoint, prints a summarized view + JSON option for attaching to tickets.
- **Metrics:**
  - `burst_explain_requests_total{status=ok|not_found|forbidden|error}`
  - `burst_explain_request_duration_ms_bucket`

## Motivation / context
We already expose denial reason codes to routing/gateway, but Support frequently needs *why* a specific code applied (cap vs cooldown vs pool_safety vs fairness) and what exact limit numbers were in effect. During beta we also saw confusion when telemetry lag caused transient fairness denials; having the telemetry age bucket in the explanation makes these cases much easier to triage.

## API details
`POST /internal/v1/burst/admission/explain`
- Request (one-of):
  - `{ request_id: "<uuid>", region: "us-east", hw_class: "h100" }`
  - `{ tenant_id: "tnt_...", region: "us-east", hw_class: "h100", model: "llama-3.1-70b", tokens_requested: 4096, is_burst: true, at_ms: 1715900000000 }`
- Response (redacted):
  - `{ decision: "denied", denial_reason_code: "BURST_CAP_REACHED", computed_limits: { ... }, evaluated_rules: ["max_burst_ratio:pass", "cooldown:pass", "cap:fail"], telemetry: { pool_utilization_age_ms_bucket: "5s-15s" }, policy_snapshot_id: "pol_2025-05-20_14-33" }`

## Security / access control
- Endpoint is only served on the internal listener.
- Requires mTLS + RBAC role `support:burst_debug:read`.
- Request payload and response are **not** logged verbatim. We log a minimal audit-style record: caller principal, time, request_id hash, and status.

## Testing
- Unit tests for:
  - redaction behavior (ensures prompts/headers never appear)
  - rule evaluation formatting
  - request_id not found path
  - RBAC gating
- Added a small integration test that mocks a stored denial decision and validates the explain response matches the stored snapshot.

## Rollout plan
- Behind feature flag `burst.support_explain_endpoint_enabled` default OFF.
- Enable for staging first; then prod (internal-only) after SRE signoff.

## Notes / follow-ups
- This PR uses an in-memory decision cache; request_id lookups can fail if the decision was made on a different node. Follow-up will add a short-lived shared store keyed by request_id (separate PR) once we’re confident about retention + PII constraints.

## Checklist
- [x] RBAC + internal listener only
- [x] Redaction tests
- [x] Runbook snippet added
- [x] Metrics added (low-cardinality)

Related: ENG-4881, PR #23795 (denial reason codes), PR #23866 (audit events for burst config changes).

title: Dedicated burst: add priority tiers (gold/silver/standard) with anti-starvation constraints
preview:
Context: Dedicated burst pools are shared across multiple tenants (within a region/hardware class). We already enforce per-tenant burst caps + cooldowns + a pool-level safety cap, but we need an explicit tiering model for Enterprise accounts (e.g., Gold/Silver) so we can preferentially admit burst while still protecting baseline tenants and preventing starvation.

This PR adds Priority Tiers to the burst admission controller fairness layer. The tier affects the tenant’s effective burst token refill rate / weight when the shared pool is contended.

Key points:
- Adds `priority_tier` to tenant burst config: `STANDARD | SILVER | GOLD`.
- Implements weighted fairness in the admission controller using tier weights (default weights: STANDARD=1.0, SILVER=1.5, GOLD=2.0) but with hard constraints:
  - Anti-starvation floor: every enabled tenant gets a minimum refill share when contended (`min_refill_share_bps`, default 250 bps) so STANDARD tenants cannot be fully starved by higher tiers.
  - Max advantage clamp: effective weight ratio is capped (`max_weight_ratio`, default 2.5) to keep GOLD from dominating when there are few tenants.
  - Burst cap always wins: tiering does not bypass per-tenant burst caps, cooldown windows, or global fleet safety caps.
- Determinism: admission decisions remain deterministic for a (tenant, pool, request_size, timestamp_bucket) input. Tier weights and constraints are part of the evaluated policy snapshot; debug endpoint includes tier + derived weight/limits.
- Observability: adds labels for tier (low-cardinality) and exports per-tier admit/deny counts and computed “fair share” gauges for debugging.

Non-goals:
- No customer-configurable custom weights (fixed mapping for GA).
- No cross-region tier borrowing.

Rollout:
- Tier field is optional; defaults to STANDARD.
- Feature is guarded behind `burst.priority_tiers_enabled` policy flag (default off) and allow-listed per region/hardware class.

Testing:
- Unit tests for weight clamp + starvation floor.
- Integration tests for multi-tenant contention scenarios: ensures STANDARD still receives admits under sustained GOLD load.

Follow-ups:
- Add Console surface for tier (read-only) once customer UI work lands.
- Revisit defaults after beta telemetry (esp. min share bps vs tail latency).

title: Add Audit Exporter Grafana dashboards + alert rules (lag, delivery errors)
preview: Context: As part of the SIEM-ready audit export beta, we need first-class operational visibility for the audit-log-exporter service (lag, retries, adapter failures) and alerting aligned to the SLOs defined by SRE. This PR adds Grafana dashboards and Prometheus alert rules for the exporter, intended to be deployed to hosted + dedicated environments for the beta cohort. Links: Linear ENG-5168 (exporter observability), Confluence SLO doc: "Audit exporter SLOs and alerting" (owner: Sean/Rafael). Scope: (1) Grafana dashboard: Overview (lag, throughput, success rate), (2) Grafana dashboard: Destination adapters (Splunk HEC / Datadog / S3), (3) Alert rules: export lag burn, sustained adapter errors, DLQ growth, auto-paused exports, (4) Recording rules for rate-based panels to reduce query cost. Notes on semantics: Export lag is computed from exporter checkpoint watermark vs event time; alerts use p95 lag by tenant and also a global max with cardinality controls. We intentionally avoid alerting on single-tenant spikes unless they violate the global budget or a configured "beta tenant allowlist" label is present. Rollout: safe to merge; rules are gated behind the existing audit-exporter job selector and will be enabled per cluster by SRE during beta rollout.

title: Add runbook links to experiment alerts + Console guardrail banners
preview: Problem: During beta we saw multiple slow triage loops where the on-call (and Support) had to ask around for the right runbook when an experiment guardrail alert fired (propagation lag, attribution drop rate, variant skew, etc.). We already have runbooks in Confluence, but the link isn’t present where the incident starts: alert payloads and the Console guardrail banners. This PR wires canonical runbook URLs into (1) alert payload annotations (so PagerDuty/Slack notifications carry a direct ‘Runbook’ link) and (2) Console guardrail banners on the experiment details/results pages. Scope: route experiments only (no global SRE alert conventions refactor in this PR). What changed: - Added a small, versioned mapping of {alert_key -> runbook_url} for experiment-related alerts (propagation lag, attribution drop rate, variant 0 traffic, allocation skew, p99 regression auto-stop). - Plumbed runbook_url through the alert/notification payload used by the guardrail evaluator and the notifications pipeline. - Updated Console guardrail banner model to accept an optional runbook_url and render a ‘View runbook’ CTA when present. - Added safeguards: if an alert key is unknown or the URL is missing/invalid, we omit the link (no broken UI, no malformed payload). - Added unit tests for mapping + URL validation and an integration test that asserts the runbook link is present end-to-end for a representative guardrail event. Runbook sources (canonical): - Experiment assignment debugging runbook: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPLAT/Experiment+Assignment+Debugging+Runbook - Experiment health dashboards spec (links out to dashboards/runbooks): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/ENGPLAT/Experiment+Health+Dashboards+Spec Notes: - This intentionally does not change Grafana rule definitions in observability-pack; it only ensures Redwood-originated notifications + Console surfaces carry the runbook URL. (Observability-pack PRs can independently add runbook_url annotations at rule level.) - URL allowlist: limited to confluence.redwoodinference.com to avoid arbitrary links in alert payloads. - Console UI is behind the existing route experiments feature flag; the banner rendering is a no-op if the backend doesn’t send runbook_url. Testing: - Unit: GuardrailAlertRunbookMapTest, UrlValidationTest - Integration: ExperimentGuardrailNotificationE2E (asserts runbook_url in notification payload + Console API response) Rollout: safe to ship with no config migrations; if we need to change runbook URLs, we can update the mapping and redeploy. Follow-ups: - Add runbook_url annotation in observability-pack alert rules so Grafana-native alerts match Redwood notifications. - Expand the mapping to cover non-experiment Rollouts alerts after GA if this proves useful.

title: Add Grafana dashboard: Route Experiment Guardrails (latency/error/cost + attribution drop)
preview:
## Summary This PR adds a standard Grafana dashboard for Route-level Experiments guardrails and data quality signals. The dashboard is intended to be deployed alongside the route experiments beta/GA so on-call and customers (where applicable) can quickly answer: (1) Is any variant regressing on latency, error rate, or cost? (2) Are we missing experiment attribution (drop rate) such that results are untrustworthy? (3) Is traffic split behaving as expected (e.g., variant getting ~0% traffic)?

This dashboard complements the control-plane/Console views by providing a fast, infrastructure-centric view over raw telemetry, and is designed to work with the canonical attribution dimensions from ADR-048 (route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region, model_id) while limiting cardinality blast-radius.

## What’s included ### Dashboard: "Route Experiments / Guardrails" Panels (high-level): - Overview row: total RPS, total requests, error rate (5xx + 429 separately), and attribution coverage.
- Latency guardrails: p50/p95/p99 request latency by variant (timeseries + stat) with a "% delta vs control" table.
- Error guardrails: error rate by variant (5xx and 429 broken out), plus "top error codes" if available.
- Cost guardrails: $/1K output tokens and $/request by variant (based on Redwood cost attribution metrics).
- Throughput: tokens/sec and requests/sec by variant (useful for distinguishing capacity issues from model behavior).
- Traffic split sanity: % of traffic per variant; includes a "variant ~0 traffic" panel to catch allocation/eligibility mistakes.
- Data quality: experiment attribution drop rate (requests missing experiment_id/variant_id when they should be present), plus a "streaming retry attribution" hint panel (links to runbook section).

### Template variables - env (prod/staging) - region - route_id (normalized route template id; NOT raw path) - experiment_id - variant_id - model_id (optional; default All)

### Data sources / metric expectations This dashboard assumes the router/runtime emits experiment dimensions on key metrics and/or spans, as implemented in the core monorepo (see redwood PRs: request context plumbing + OTel tagging). The queries here are written to tolerate partial rollout by falling back to "All variants" if variant tags are absent.

Primary metrics used (names reflect current conventions in the pack; see README changes for exact mapping): - redwood_request_latency_ms_bucket{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region} - redwood_requests_total{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region, status_class} - redwood_tokens_output_total{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region} - redwood_cost_usd_total{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region} - redwood_experiment_attribution_missing_total{route_id, region, reason} and redwood_requests_total (used as denominator)

## Why / context - We’ve had multiple beta issues where Console results were confusing or appeared stale, and on-call needed a quick way to validate (a) attribution integrity and (b) whether a regression was real vs. a data artifact (ref: SUP-24910).
- SRE asked for a standardized dashboard that can be installed for every region/environment with consistent panels and variable naming so runbooks/alerts can deep-link reliably.
- The attribution drop rate panels are explicitly included because missing experiment_id/variant_id has been a recurring failure mode (streaming retries, async spans, and header/sticky-key issues).

## Implementation notes - Cardinality: the dashboard does NOT group by raw request path. It uses normalized route_id (template) and requires users to select an experiment_id for most by-variant panels. We avoid “group by {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region}” in single queries unless the user is already scoped down.
- Cost metrics: panels use $/1K output tokens and $/request. This avoids misleading comparisons for variants with different output length distributions.

text:
## Summary This PR adds a standard Grafana dashboard for Route-level Experiments guardrails and data quality signals. The dashboard is intended to be deployed alongside the route experiments beta/GA so on-call and customers (where applicable) can quickly answer: (1) Is any variant regressing on latency, error rate, or cost? (2) Are we missing experiment attribution (drop rate) such that results are untrustworthy? (3) Is traffic split behaving as expected (e.g., variant getting ~0% traffic)?

This dashboard complements the control-plane/Console views by providing a fast, infrastructure-centric view over raw telemetry, and is designed to work with the canonical attribution dimensions from ADR-048 (route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region, model_id) while limiting cardinality blast-radius.

## What’s included ### Dashboard: "Route Experiments / Guardrails" Panels (high-level): - Overview row: total RPS, total requests, error rate (5xx + 429 separately), and attribution coverage.
- Latency guardrails: p50/p95/p99 request latency by variant (timeseries + stat) with a "% delta vs control" table.
- Error guardrails: error rate by variant (5xx and 429 broken out), plus "top error codes" if available.
- Cost guardrails: $/1K output tokens and $/request by variant (based on Redwood cost attribution metrics).
- Throughput: tokens/sec and requests/sec by variant (useful for distinguishing capacity issues from model behavior).
- Traffic split sanity: % of traffic per variant; includes a "variant ~0 traffic" panel to catch allocation/eligibility mistakes.
- Data quality: experiment attribution drop rate (requests missing experiment_id/variant_id when they should be present), plus a "streaming retry attribution" hint panel (links to runbook section).

### Template variables - env (prod/staging) - region - route_id (normalized route template id; NOT raw path) - experiment_id - variant_id - model_id (optional; default All)

### Data sources / metric expectations This dashboard assumes the router/runtime emits experiment dimensions on key metrics and/or spans, as implemented in the core monorepo (see redwood PRs: request context plumbing + OTel tagging). The queries here are written to tolerate partial rollout by falling back to "All variants" if variant tags are absent.

Primary metrics used (names reflect current conventions in the pack; see README changes for exact mapping): - redwood_request_latency_ms_bucket{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region} - redwood_requests_total{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region, status_class} - redwood_tokens_output_total{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region} - redwood_cost_usd_total{route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region} - redwood_experiment_attribution_missing_total{route_id, region, reason} and redwood_requests_total (used as denominator)

## Why / context - We’ve had multiple beta issues where Console results were confusing or appeared stale, and on-call needed a quick way to validate (a) attribution integrity and (b) whether a regression was real vs. a data artifact (ref: SUP-24910).
- SRE asked for a standardized dashboard that can be installed for every region/environment with consistent panels and variable naming so runbooks/alerts can deep-link reliably.
- The attribution drop rate panels are explicitly included because missing experiment_id/variant_id has been a recurring failure mode (streaming retries, async spans, and header/sticky-key issues).

## Implementation notes - Cardinality: the dashboard does NOT group by raw request path. It uses normalized route_id (template) and requires users to select an experiment_id for most by-variant panels. We avoid “group by {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region}” in single queries unless the user is already scoped down.
- Cost metrics: panels use $/1K output tokens and $/request. This avoids misleading comparisons for variants with different output length distributions.
- Control variant: the “delta vs control” table assumes variant_id="control" when present; otherwise it picks the lowest-sorted variant_id as a fallback. This is called out in the panel description so it doesn’t silently mislead.

title: Feature flag + cohort targeting for Incident Bot v2 diagnostics output
preview: Summary: Introduces a gated rollout mechanism for Incident Bot v2 diagnostics (deploy context/config diffs/saturation signals) so we can run a limited beta (SRE rotation + selected regions/channels) and safely roll back to v1 output if bot latency or upstream query cost spikes during incidents. Motivation: v2 diagnostics increases upstream call volume and payload size; we need controlled exposure + a kill switch to avoid self-inflicted load during Sev events. Implementation details: Adds a new feature-flag abstraction (DiagV2Gate) with cohort targeting on Slack context (workspace, channel, user group), incident context (env/region/service), and optional allow/deny lists. Default behavior is OFF unless explicitly enabled; bot continues to render v1 cards with deep links. Adds three evaluation modes: (1) global enable/disable (INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_ENABLED), (2) cohort allowlist (INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_COHORTS), (3) per-thread override for on-call testing via a Slack interactive action "Enable v2 diagnostics in this thread" which expires automatically after a configurable TTL. Rollback/safety: Adds a hard kill switch (INCIDENT_BOT_DIAG_V2_KILL_SWITCH) that forces v1 regardless of cohort evaluation; also adds budgeted evaluation logging + metric counters to confirm flag decisions during incidents. The bot will fall back to v1 output if upstream v2 context gathering times out, but still logs the flag decision (without leaking any tenant identifiers). Telemetry: Emits metrics incident_bot.diag_v2.gate_decision{decision=enabled|disabled, reason=...} and incident_bot.diag_v2.thread_override{action=enabled|disabled}. Docs: Updates operator notes in /docs to describe how to configure cohorts and how to disable v2 during an incident. Testing: Unit tests cover cohort parsing, precedence rules (kill switch > per-thread override > denylist > allowlist > global), and TTL expiration. Includes a simple integration test that simulates message events from #incidents and validates the correct renderer path is taken. Rollout plan: Start with SRE rotation channels only (#incidents, #eng-oncall) in staging; enable prod for 10% cohort (SRE on-call user group) for one rotation; expand to platform on-call after telemetry confirms stable bot response times and no noticeable increase in diagnostics endpoint QPS. Related: ENG-5428 (rollout + feature-flagged beta), ENG-5425 (Slack interaction reliability/acks).

title: Alerts: experiment config propagation lag + attribution anomalies (route experiments)
preview: Context: Route-level experiments rely on control-plane config propagation into the routing/policy layer. During beta we observed occasional propagation lag and a few classes of attribution anomalies (missing experiment_id/variant_id tags, variant receiving ~0 traffic due to config/eligibility issues, and split skew early in rollout). This PR adds Prometheus/Alertmanager rules and annotations for the standard route experiments dashboards, with SRE-reviewed runbook links and recommended severities. Scope: (1) propagation lag/staleness alerts tied to the new propagation lag metric emitted by the redwood monorepo, (2) attribution drop-rate and missing-dimension alerts for experiment metrics/traces aggregation, (3) “variant 0 traffic” detection to catch misconfigured allocations or eligibility rules. Notes: rules are written to avoid cardinality blow-ups (no per-route fanout by default); high-cardinality drilldowns remain in Grafana dashboards. Related: redwood PR-28631 (adds config propagation lag metric), redwood PR-28734 (runbook URLs in alert payloads), observability-pack PR-1934 (dashboards). Threads: slack/eng-sre/1736455521-alert-thresholds-experiment-propagation-lag.json and slack/eng-sre/1738362044-attribution-drop-rate-alert-noise.json informed thresholds/windows. Rollout: enable in staging immediately; prod enabled behind the existing observability-pack release gate (see release notes section).
